La ley establecerá la coordinación suficiente para evitar procedimientos contradictorios o duplicativos sobre la misma conducta. 5.3 Todo funcionario federal —incluidos el Presidente, cualquier miembro del Congreso o cualquier juez federal— está sujeto a investigación y procesamiento conforme a este Artículo. Ningún cargo otorga inmunidad frente a estas disposiciones. 5.4 La facultad de la Comisión de Rendición de Cuentas para investigar y procesar las infracciones de este Artículo forma parte del núcleo inalterable del Artículo XI. No puede ser reducida, limitada ni suprimida mediante enmienda constitucional. Si la Comisión de Rendición de Cuentas llegara a ser sistemáticamente capturada o dejara de cumplir sus funciones esenciales durante un período prolongado, el Tribunal Supremo podrá declararla en situación de fracaso institucional y activar un proceso de reconstitución establecido por la ley, compatible con la independencia judicial y la continuidad constitucional.

docs.title: Artículo IX — Ciudadanía y Membresía Nacional
docs.summary: **Estado:** `[ BORRADOR ]`

headings.text: Sección 4 — Igual Elegibilidad para los Cargos Públicos
Sección 5 — Pérdida, Renuncia y Conflicto de Membresía

docs.search_text: Artículo IX — Ciudadanía y Membresía Nacional Estado: [ BORRADOR ] Ámbito Establece la ciudadanía igualitaria, la membresía nacional, los principios de domicilio, la participación política en el extranjero y las normas constitucionales relativas al estatus de ciudadanía. La ciudadanía es un estatus jurídico en virtud de esta Constitución, pero también es una garantía contra la casta hereditaria, la exclusión arbitraria y la membresía desigual en la comunidad política. Sección 1 — Ciudadanía Igualitaria 1.1 Todos los ciudadanos de los Estados Unidos son iguales en estatus constitucional. No se establecerá distinción alguna entre ciudadanos por nacimiento y ciudadanos por naturalización, salvo cuando esta Constitución lo disponga expresamente. 1.2 La ciudadanía conlleva igual protección de las leyes, igual condición cívica e igual elegibilidad para la vida pública, sujeta únicamente a las calificaciones expresamente establecidas por esta Constitución. 1.3 Ninguna ley podrá crear rangos de ciudadanía, privilegio cívico hereditario o discapacidad cívica permanente incompatibles con esta Constitución. 1.4 La ciudadanía por nacimiento o naturalización será definida por ley de conformidad con esta Constitución. A ninguna persona nacida o naturalizada bajo las leyes de los Estados Unidos podrá negársele igual condición constitucional una vez que la ciudadanía haya sido reconocida lícitamente. 1.5 El Congreso establecerá por ley una norma uniforme de naturalización compatible con la ciudadanía igualitaria, el debido proceso y esta Constitución. Dicha ley podrá distinguir entre solicitantes según la residencia lícita, los vínculos demostrados u otros criterios constitucionalmente suficientes, pero no podrá hacer que la naturalización sea permanentemente inaccesible para ninguna clase de personas, salvo por motivos compatibles con esta Constitución. Sección 2 — Membresía Política y Domicilio 2.1 La ciudadanía es el fundamento de la membresía política nacional en virtud de esta Constitución; sin embargo, el voto, la candidatura y la representación podrán asignarse según el domicilio, la residencia u otros criterios constitucionales establecidos por esta Constitución o por ley compatible con ella. 2.2 El Congreso y la Comisión Electoral establecerán normas uniformes sobre domicilio, residencia, asignación de voto en el extranjero y prueba de ciudadanía suficientes para asegurar la membresía nacional igualitaria sin exclusión arbitraria. 2.3 Ningún ciudadano podrá ser privado de la participación electoral federal únicamente por residir en el extranjero, haber nacido en el extranjero o haber cambiado de domicilio dentro de los Estados Unidos, salvo en los casos expresamente permitidos por esta Constitución. 2.4 Ningún ciudadano podrá ser asignado simultáneamente a más de un domicilio electoral estatal o federal para la misma elección. El Congreso y la Comisión Electoral establecerán normas uniformes para prevenir la duplicación, la manipulación y la privación del derecho al voto en la asignación de domicilio. 2.5 El ciudadano que regrese a residir en los Estados Unidos recuperará la plena membresía política conforme al domicilio establecido al momento del regreso. El tiempo transcurrido en el extranjero podrá afectar la asignación electoral únicamente en los términos previstos por ley compatible con este Artículo y no podrá desvincularlo permanentemente de la membresía política nacional. Sección 3 — Ciudadanos en el Extranjero 3.1 Todo ciudadano de los Estados Unidos residente fuera del país conserva el derecho a participar en las elecciones federales. 3.2 El Congreso y la Comisión Electoral podrán distinguir por ley entre la residencia temporal y la residencia prolongada en el extranjero a efectos del voto asignado a un estado y del voto exclusivamente federal, siempre que ningún ciudadano sea privado totalmente de su participación en las elecciones federales por razón de residencia en el extranjero. El servicio militar, el servicio diplomático y el servicio público comparable prestados en el extranjero no podrán ser considerados abandono de la membresía política nacional. 3.3 Los ciudadanos que nunca hayan establecido domicilio en un estado serán asignados por ley a un domicilio electoral federal válido constitucionalmente para su participación en las elecciones federales. Dicha asignación podrá vincularse al último domicilio del progenitor ciudadano, a otro principio de conexión lícito o a un registro federal de ciudadanos en el extranjero, pero no podrá dejar al ciudadano sin un domicilio electoral federal efectivo. 3.4 El Congreso y la Comisión Electoral establecerán sistemas seguros de registro en el extranjero, envío de boletas y devolución de las mismas, suficientes para hacer efectivo y oportuno el derecho garantizado por esta Sección. 3.5 El Congreso podrá distinguir entre categorías de ciudadanos en el extranjero únicamente a efectos de asignación electoral, administración de boletas y seguridad. Ninguna de tales distinciones podrá extinguir totalmente la participación del ciudadano en las elecciones federales. Sección 4 — Igual Elegibilidad para los Cargos Públicos 4.1 Salvo disposición contraria de esta Constitución, los ciudadanos naturalizados y los ciudadanos por nacimiento son igualmente elegibles para los cargos federales. 4.2 Los requisitos adicionales de lealtad, ciudadanía exclusiva, servicio exterior o divulgación de información para determinados cargos son válidos únicamente cuando esta Constitución los prevea expresamente o cuando estén estrictamente limitados por ley a cargos que entrañen una responsabilidad de seguridad nacional de carácter excepcional. 4.3 Ninguna ley podrá imponer una incapacidad general a los ciudadanos naturalizados para ocupar cargos federales. 4.4 Cuando otro Artículo imponga calificaciones específicas para un cargo, incompatibilidades, deberes de divulgación o restricciones de nacionalidad, dichos requisitos se interpretarán de manera restrictiva y no se ampliarán por implicación a fin de establecer una jerarquía general de ciudadanía. 4.5 Las normas específicas para un cargo relativas a la nacionalidad, la lealtad o la divulgación de información aplicables a cargos de especial sensibilidad deberán interpretarse como excepciones a la ciudadanía igualitaria, y no como una autorización general para establecer una condición cívica jerarquizada. Sección 5 — Pérdida, Renuncia y Conflicto de Membresía 5.1 El Congreso podrá establecer por ley la renuncia voluntaria a la ciudadanía de los Estados Unidos y los procedimientos administrativos necesarios para reconocer dicha renuncia. 5.2 Ningún ciudadano perderá su ciudadanía salvo por renuncia voluntaria realizada con conocimiento e intencionalidad, o por resolución judicial que constate que la persona obtuvo la ciudadanía mediante fraude material y con pleno conocimiento de ello. Todo procedimiento de revocación de la ciudadanía deberá garantizar notificación, una audiencia sustantiva ante un tribunal independiente, el derecho a asistencia letrada y revisión judicial sobre el fondo antes de que la revocación surta efecto. Ninguna ley podrá prever la pérdida involuntaria de la ciudadanía como sanción, exclusión política o incapacidad civil, ni reducir las garantías procesales exigidas por esta Sección. 5.3 El Congreso podrá regular la doble ciudadanía, la fidelidad a potencias extranjeras y la incompatibilidad con cargos extranjeros para los titulares de cargos federales sensibles, pero no podrá imponer incapacidades cívicas a los ciudadanos ordinarios únicamente por ser titulares de otra nacionalidad. 5.4 Ningún ciudadano será dejado en situación de apatridia por acción gubernamental involuntaria. Toda ley que regule la pérdida, la renuncia o la suspensión del estatus de ciudadanía deberá interpretarse de modo que se evite la apatridia, salvo cuando el ciudadano haya renunciado voluntariamente y con pleno conocimiento a la ciudadanía de los Estados Unidos. Sección 6 — Prueba y Administración 6.1 El Congreso establecerá por ley procedimientos justos, uniformes y revisables para acreditar la ciudadanía cuando esta sea relevante para el ejercicio del voto, la ocupación de cargos públicos u otro estatus constitucional. 6.2 Ningún ciudadano podrá ser privado de sus derechos políticos por error administrativo sin notificación oportuna, una oportunidad sustantiva de impugnar la determinación y revisión judicial. 6.3 Ningún organismo podrá imponer cargas documentales, presunciones probatorias o prácticas administrativas que hagan prácticamente imposible acreditar la ciudadanía para las personas ordinariamente elegibles. 6.4 La revisión judicial en virtud de este Artículo será expedita cuando la demora pudiera menoscabar el ejercicio del voto, la candidatura, la ocupación de cargos públicos, los viajes u otro estatus constitucional sujeto a plazos. Sección 7 — Interpretación 7.1 Este Artículo se interpretará de manera que preserve la ciudadanía igualitaria, la membresía política efectiva y la inclusión democrática. 7.2 Toda ambigüedad en virtud de este Artículo se resolverá en contra del privilegio hereditario, la jerarquía cívica de carácter similar a una casta y la exclusión arbitraria de la membresía nacional.

docs.title: Article X — Federalism and the States
docs.summary: Establishes the division of authority between the federal government and the states. The United States is a federal republic in which the national government and state governments each possess defined sovereign authority. Neither is a mere creation of the other. This Article replaces the prior Constitution's Articles IV and VI and the Tenth Amendment.

headings.text: Section 2 — Powers of the Federal Government
Section 3 — Powers Reserved to the States
Section 6 — Washington D.C. and the National Capital
Section 7 — Territories and Admission of New States

docs.search_text: Article X — Federalism and the States Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the division of authority between the federal government and the states. The United States is a federal republic in which the national government and state governments each possess defined sovereign authority. Neither is a mere creation of the other. This Article replaces the prior Constitution's Articles IV and VI and the Tenth Amendment. Section 1 — Principles of Federalism 1.1 The powers of the federal government are those granted by this Constitution, expressly or by necessary implication. All other powers are reserved to the states or to the people. 1.2 State governments are sovereign within their reserved domains. The federal government may not commandeer state legislatures, state executives, or state courts to implement federal programs or enforce federal law against state will, except where this Constitution expressly provides otherwise. 1.3 The federal government and the states are partners in governance, not adversaries. Both are obligated to act in good faith toward the citizens they serve and toward each other. 1.4 Rights floor. The rights guaranteed by Article V are a minimum floor. States may enact laws that expand rights beyond the federal floor. No state may enact laws that reduce rights below the federal floor. Where a state law provides greater protection for a right than Article V requires, the state standard governs within that state. 1.5 Democratic floor. Every state shall maintain a republican form of government including free and fair elections, an independent judiciary, and separation of executive and legislative power. The federal government shall guarantee this form of government to every state and shall protect each state against invasion and, upon application of the state legislature or executive, against domestic violence. 1.6 — Enforcement of the democratic floor. Where a state persistently and materially violates the democratic floor of Section 1.5, the following enforcement mechanism applies: (a) Finding. The Supreme Court, on petition by the United States or by the legislature or executive of any other state, may find that a state is in persistent and material violation of Section 1.5. The finding must identify the constitutional violations and explain why ordinary judicial relief has proved insufficient. (b) Congressional remedy. Upon a finding under Section 1.6(a), Congress shall within 180 days enact a remedial measure reasonably directed to restoring constitutional government in the state. Such measures may include federal administration of federal elections in the state, conditions on federal grants related to the violation, or other lawful means consistent with this Constitution. (c) Automatic interim remedy and mandatory suspension vote after remedial failure. If Congress fails to enact a remedial measure within the 180 day period of Section 1.6(b), federal elections conducted in the state shall immediately be administered under federally supervised procedures established by law and carried out through the Electoral Commission until Congress acts or the Supreme Court certifies compliance. During the same period, the House and the Regional Assembly shall each hold a recorded vote within the next 30 days on whether to suspend the state's representation until compliance is restored. Members from the affected state may not vote on that question, and the denominator shall be reduced accordingly. If either chamber fails to hold the required vote within that 30 day period, the state's representation in that chamber is automatically suspended as of the thirty first day, unless 2/3 of the full membership of that chamber has voted within the 30 day period to expressly decline suspension. The supermajority threshold ensures that automatic suspension reflects genuine legislative inaction rather than leadership or quorum obstruction, while making it meaningfully difficult to escape a consequence that follows a Supreme Court finding and a missed congressional remedy. (d) Suspension of representation. If the state remains in material violation one year after a Supreme Court finding under Section 1.6(a), the state's representation in the House and Regional Assembly is suspended automatically until the Supreme Court certifies compliance, unless Congress has already imposed suspension earlier under Section 1.6(c) or has enacted a remedial measure that the Supreme Court finds is producing substantial and timely progress toward compliance. Congress may extend that progress determination once for no more than 90 days by a 2/3 vote of both chambers. Suspension does not affect the constitutional rights of the state's residents, does not expel the state from the Union, and does not create a permanent blocking minority in the amendment process under Article XI. (e) Restoration. Representation shall be restored immediately upon Supreme Court certification that the state has returned to compliance with Section 1.5. 1.7 — Electoral integrity floor for state elections. Free and fair elections require, at minimum, that the identity of those funding candidates and campaigns be known to voters, that foreign money be excluded, and that the political spending of corporations and similar entities not be treated as equivalent to the political expression of citizens. These requirements are conditions of the democratic floor of Section 1.5. Every state shall maintain the following minimum standards for all elections conducted within its borders, including state and local elections: (a) Disclosure. All contributions and expenditures made for the purpose of influencing a state or local election shall be publicly disclosed. Disclosure shall identify the original human source of funds through all intermediaries. No entity may be used as a conduit to conceal the identity of the original human donor. A state may set stricter disclosure requirements than this floor but may not set weaker ones. (b) Anonymous contributions. No anonymous contribution above two hundred dollars may be made to any candidate, political committee, or entity making expenditures to influence a state or local election. States may lower but not raise this threshold. (c) Foreign money. No foreign national, foreign government, foreign corporation, or entity owned or controlled by foreign interests may contribute to or make expenditures in connection with any state or local election. This prohibition applies regardless of whether funds are routed through a domestic intermediary. (d) Corporate and organizational spending. Corporations, labor unions, trade associations, and other legal entities are not natural persons and do not possess the same constitutional rights as individual citizens with respect to political spending in state and local elections. States may regulate or prohibit their political expenditures without satisfying strict scrutiny. (e) Government contractors. No entity holding an active state or local government contract above a threshold set by state law may make political contributions or expenditures in elections for offices that exercise regulatory or contracting authority over that entity. States shall set the specific threshold and coverage by law but may not exempt state contractors from this principle entirely. (f) Cryptocurrency and digital assets. No contribution or expenditure to influence a state or local election may be made through a cryptocurrency or digital asset unless the full identity chain from the originating wallet to an identified natural person can be verified and disclosed in compliance with the disclosure requirement of Section 1.7(a). (g) Public financing option. Every state shall make available at least one public financing option for candidates for statewide office. The design, funding level, and matching structure are matters of state law; the obligation to provide an option is a condition of the democratic floor. (h) Enforcement. A state that persistently and materially fails to maintain the standards of this Section is in violation of the democratic floor of Section 1.5 and subject to the enforcement mechanism of Section 1.6. The Accountability Commission may refer evidence of systematic violations to the Supreme Court. 1.8 — Federal incentive for full electoral integrity compliance. A state that voluntarily adopts campaign finance standards equivalent to or stricter than the full framework of Article VII — including outside spending caps, per actor aggregate limits, and the geographic concentration limit — shall be eligible to participate in the federal public financing matching infrastructure administered by the Electoral Commission. Participation allows state candidates to access federal matching fund infrastructure for qualifying small dollar contributions in state races, at the state's option and cost. This creates a positive incentive for states to go beyond the floor without requiring them to do so. Section 2 — Powers of the Federal Government Congress shall have the power to: (a) Defense and security. Raise, maintain, and regulate the armed forces; declare war; provide for the common defense; establish rules for the conduct of warfare; enter into alliances and mutual defense treaties. (b) Foreign affairs. Conduct foreign policy; negotiate and ratify treaties; regulate relations with foreign governments and international organizations; define and punish offenses against the law of nations. (c) Commerce. Regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the states, and with Indian nations. This power includes the authority to regulate activities that substantially affect interstate commerce, establish rules for interstate transactions, and prevent states from erecting barriers to interstate trade. It does not include the power to compel individuals to engage in commerce. (d) Taxation and spending. Lay and collect taxes, duties, and imposts; borrow money on the credit of the United States; spend for the general welfare of the United States. Federal spending may attach conditions to grants to states, provided those conditions are clearly stated, directly related to the purpose of the grant, and not so coercive as to leave states no meaningful choice. (e) Currency and financial system. Coin money, regulate its value, and maintain the integrity of the monetary system; charter and regulate banks and financial institutions engaged in interstate activity; establish bankruptcy laws. (f) Immigration and naturalization. Establish uniform rules for immigration and the naturalization of citizens. States may not enact immigration enforcement regimes that conflict with federal immigration law, but may enact laws that extend benefits or protections to immigrants beyond the federal floor. (g) Civil rights. Enforce the rights guaranteed by this Constitution against infringement by state governments or private actors, through appropriate legislation. (h) Environment and natural resources. Regulate pollution, protect navigable waters, manage federal lands, and address environmental harms that cross state boundaries or affect the global commons. States may enact environmental standards stricter than federal standards. (i) Public health. Respond to public health emergencies that cross state lines; regulate the safety of food, drugs, and medical devices in interstate commerce; fund and coordinate national public health systems. (j) Infrastructure. Build and maintain infrastructure of national significance, including interstate highways, rail networks, air traffic systems, and communications infrastructure. (k) Science and knowledge. Fund scientific research; establish standards of weights and measures; grant patents and copyrights for limited terms to promote innovation and the useful arts. (l) Federal courts. Establish courts inferior to the Supreme Court, define their jurisdiction, and fund their operation. (m) Federal lands and territories. Exercise exclusive jurisdiction over federal lands, installations, and territories not admitted as states. (n) Postal and communications. Establish postal services and regulate interstate communications networks. (o) Necessary and proper. Make all laws necessary and proper for carrying into execution the foregoing powers and all other powers vested in the federal government by this Constitution. This power authorizes means reasonably adapted to enumerated ends; it does not create independent powers beyond those enumerated. The Necessary and Proper Clause may not be invoked to regulate activity that is (i) purely intrastate, (ii) not economic in nature, and (iii) has no substantial effect on interstate commerce; all three conditions must be present for this limitation to apply. Where invocation of the Clause would expand an enumerated power beyond its historical scope, courts shall apply heightened scrutiny, requiring the government to demonstrate a close and direct relationship between the means chosen and an enumerated end. Section 3 — Powers Reserved to the States 3.1 Powers not granted to the federal government by this Constitution, and not prohibited to the states, are reserved to the states or to the people. 3.2 The reserved powers of the states include, without limitation: (a) Criminal law — defining and punishing offenses against state law, operating police and corrections systems, subject to the rights guaranteed by Article V (b) Education — operating and funding public education above the federal floor established by Article XVIII, Section 2.2 (c) Land use and zoning — regulating the use of land within the state, including housing, development, and environmental conservation beyond federal minimums (d) Family law — regulating marriage, divorce, adoption, and child welfare, within the rights guaranteed by Article V (e) Professional licensing — establishing standards for occupations practiced within the state, subject to interstate recognition requirements of Section 5 (f) State taxation — levying taxes on residents and activity within the state, subject to the prohibition on burdening interstate commerce (g) Public health and safety — regulating health, safety, and welfare within the state, subject to federal preemption where applicable (h) Local government — creating, organizing, and empowering municipal governments, subject to the local government protections of Section 8 3.3 Laboratories of democracy. States are encouraged to experiment with innovative policies within their reserved domains. The federal government shall not preempt state policy experimentation except where the experiment creates demonstrable harm to residents of other states or violates the requirements of this Constitution. Where a state law causes demonstrable harm to residents of another state — including environmental contamination, financial fraud, or public health risks — the affected state has standing to sue the offending state in federal court without further jurisdictional prerequisites. Courts adjudicating such suits shall apply a rebuttable presumption that cross border harms of this nature are preemptable by the affected state's interests and by federal law; the offending state bears the burden of rebutting that presumption. Section 4 — Federal Preemption 4.1 Express preemption. Where Congress explicitly states that federal law preempts state law on a subject, federal law governs. Preemption clauses shall be construed narrowly — a statement that federal law governs a subject does not preempt state laws that supplement or exceed federal standards unless Congress explicitly says so. 4.2 Conflict preemption. Where compliance with both federal and state law is physically impossible, or where state law directly obstructs the achievement of a federal statutory purpose, federal law governs to the extent of the conflict. State law that merely exceeds federal standards does not conflict with federal law. 4.3 No implied field preemption. Federal regulation of a subject does not automatically preempt state regulation of the same subject. States may regulate concurrently with the federal government in most domains. Implied field preemption — the doctrine that federal regulation of an area implicitly occupies the entire field — is abolished except in the following domains where uniform national policy is constitutionally essential: (a) Foreign affairs and relations with foreign governments (b) National security and the conduct of warfare (c) Currency and monetary policy (d) Immigration status and naturalization 4.4 The concurrent regulation principle. In all areas not subject to implied field preemption under Section 4.3, states may regulate more strictly than federal standards but may not regulate less strictly. A state environmental standard more stringent than federal law is valid. A state environmental standard less stringent than federal law is invalid to the extent of the conflict. 4.5 Anti circumvention. Congress may not use conditional grants, regulatory mandates, or other indirect means to effectively commandeer state governments to implement federal programs when direct federal action would be constitutionally impermissible. Section 5 — Interstate Relations 5.1 Full faith and credit. Each state shall give full faith and credit to the public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of every other state. In particular: (a) A marriage or civil union lawfully entered into in any state shall be recognized as valid in every other state, regardless of whether the receiving state would have authorized such a marriage under its own law (b) A final judgment of a court of any state shall be recognized and enforced in every other state, subject to the requirement that the rendering court had proper jurisdiction (c) A professional license issued by any state, upon meeting minimum federal qualification standards established by Congress, shall be recognized as valid in every other state for the practice of that profession. States may impose additional requirements for practice within the state but may not refuse to recognize the base qualification 5.2 Extradition. A person charged with a crime in one state who is found in another state shall be delivered to the charging state upon the demand of the executive authority of that state. The governor of the receiving state shall issue the warrant of extradition without inquiry into the merits of the charges, subject to the following. Extradition requests are subject to judicial review in the sending (receiving) state: a court of competent jurisdiction in the state where the person is found may deny extradition if it finds, by clear and convincing evidence, that the requesting state's charges are pretextual, politically motivated, or facially unconstitutional. A denial under this provision is subject to immediate appeal; the court must issue its ruling within 30 days of the extradition request being contested. 5.3 Interstate compacts. Two or more states may enter into compacts with each other on any matter within their reserved powers. Compacts that affect the powers of the federal government or the rights of states not party to the compact require the consent of Congress. 5.4 Prohibition on interstate trade barriers. No state shall impose tariffs, taxes, or discriminatory regulations on goods, services, or persons moving in interstate commerce. States may regulate commerce within their borders for legitimate health, safety, and welfare purposes, provided such regulations do not discriminate against out of state commerce and do not impose an undue burden on interstate trade. 5.5 Cross border reach of state law. No state may enforce its laws against conduct that occurred entirely within another state, or against a person for actions taken in another state where those actions were lawful. A state's criminal and civil law applies to conduct within its borders; it does not apply to lawful conduct by its residents in other states. For purposes of digital activity, conduct is deemed to occur where the actor is physically located at the time of the conduct, not where the effects of that conduct are felt. A state may not assert jurisdiction over digital conduct that originates outside its borders solely on the basis of the conduct's audience reach within the state or its downstream effects on persons located within the state; additional nexus to the state beyond mere audience or effect is required to support jurisdiction. Section 6 — Washington D.C. and the National Capital 6.1 The seat of the federal government shall be a defined area designated the National Capital District, comprising the United States Capitol building and grounds, the White House and Executive Office complex, the Supreme Court building, and such federal facilities in close proximity as Congress designates. The National Capital District shall be governed exclusively by Congress. 6.2 All territory of the current District of Columbia not included within the National Capital District under Section 6.1 shall be admitted as a state, to be known as the State of Washington, and shall be represented in the House of Representatives, the Regional Assembly, and all other federal institutions on the same basis as any other state. 6.3 Residents of the National Capital District who do not reside in the State of Washington shall be treated as residents of the State of Washington for purposes of federal elections. Section 7 — Territories and Admission of New States 7.1 Citizenship. All persons born in any territory, possession, or commonwealth of the United States are citizens of the United States with full constitutional rights, regardless of whether their place of birth has been admitted as a state. 7.2 Right to petition for statehood. Any territory, possession, or commonwealth of the United States with a resident population of at least 100,000 citizens may petition Congress for admission as a state. Congress shall act on any such petition within five years of its submission, either by admitting the territory as a state, by offering a schedule of admission, or by providing written reasons for declining and specifying what conditions, if any, would need to be met for admission. 7.3 Right to self determination. The residents of any territory, possession, or commonwealth may vote in a referendum on their preferred political status, including statehood, independence, free association, or continuation of current status. Congress shall recognize the result of any such referendum and shall take action consistent with it within a reasonable period. 7.4 Admission of new states. New states may be admitted by Congress. No new state shall be formed within the jurisdiction of any existing state, or by the junction of two or more states, without the consent of the legislatures of the states concerned as well as Congress. 7.5 Federal territories. Congress shall exercise authority over all territories not admitted as states. Residents of territories shall have the right to local self government and to all rights guaranteed by Article V. Section 8 — Local Government 8.1 Right to local self government. Every city, town, or municipality with a resident population of at least 10,000 citizens, and every county with a resident population of at least 50,000 citizens, has the right to local self government, including the right to elect its own governing body, levy local taxes within state set limits, and enact ordinances governing local matters. Special districts and single purpose administrative bodies are not general purpose governments and do not possess the rights established by this Section; their structure and autonomy are governed by state law. 8.2 Protection from state override. A state may not override, preempt, or nullify a local ordinance on a matter of purely local concern — defined as a matter whose effects are substantially confined to the jurisdiction of the local government — unless the state demonstrates a compelling statewide interest that cannot be served by less intrusive means. In any proceeding to enforce this Section, the state must demonstrate by clear and convincing evidence that the local ordinance conflicts with a specific and articulable statewide interest; speculative, generalized, or post hoc rationalizations do not qualify as sufficient statewide interests. Courts shall apply heightened scrutiny to state preemption of local housing, labor, and environmental regulations, requiring the state to identify the precise conflict between the local ordinance and the statewide interest and to show that preemption is the least restrictive means of resolving that conflict. 8.3 Matters of statewide concern. Where a matter has significant effects beyond a single municipality — including land use decisions affecting regional housing availability, environmental regulation, and infrastructure — states may establish minimum standards that localities must meet. Localities may exceed but not fall below those standards. 8.4 No abolition. A state may restructure, merge, or reorganize local governments for legitimate governmental purposes. A state may not abolish all forms of local self government within any populated area, or so thoroughly preempt local authority as to render local government meaningless. Section 9 — Federal Supremacy 9.1 This Constitution and the laws and treaties of the United States made pursuant to it are the supreme law of the land. Judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in any state constitution or law to the contrary notwithstanding. 9.2 Federal supremacy applies only to federal actions taken within the scope of powers granted by this Constitution. An unconstitutional federal act does not override state law by virtue of the Supremacy Clause — federal supremacy flows from constitutional authority, not from the mere fact of federal action. 9.3 No state or local official is required to enforce federal law. The federal government may enforce its own laws through its own officers and courts. A state's refusal to assist federal enforcement does not nullify federal law within that state but does not expose the state to penalty solely on account of that refusal.

docs.title: Article XI — Amendments
docs.summary: Establishes the processes by which this Constitution may be amended, the provisions that may never be amended, and the judicial mechanisms that enforce both.
docs.search_text: Article XI — Amendments Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the processes by which this Constitution may be amended, the provisions that may never be amended, and the judicial mechanisms that enforce both. Section 1 — General Principle This Constitution is a living framework intended to endure across generations. It must be capable of legitimate change while remaining resistant to capture, dismantlement, or abuse by any temporary majority. These amendment processes are designed to make necessary change achievable and democratic destruction impossible. Section 2 — Two Track Amendment Process Track 1 — Structural Amendments Applies to changes affecting the structure or powers of the Legislative, Executive, or Judicial branches, the relationship between federal and state governments, or the composition or mandate of Constitutional Organs. Process: 1. Proposed by 2/3 vote of both the House of Representatives and the Regional Assembly, in separate votes held no less than 90 days apart 2. Ratified by legislatures of 3/4 of the states within 7 years of proposal 3. Submitted to the Supreme Court for pre clearance review before ratification votes begin (see Section 4) 4. Takes effect 1 year after certification of ratification, unless the amendment specifies otherwise Track 2 — Rights Amendments Applies to the expansion of rights enumerated in Article V, or the addition of new rights not currently enumerated. May not be used to reduce, restrict, or remove any existing right. Process: 1. Proposed by 2/3 vote of both the House of Representatives and the Regional Assembly 2. Ratified by legislatures of a majority of states within 5 years of proposal 3. Confirmed by a national referendum administered by the Electoral Commission, requiring a majority of votes cast, with participation from at least 50% of registered voters 4. Submitted to the Supreme Court for pre clearance review before ratification votes begin (see Section 4) 5. Takes effect 6 months after certification of ratification Shared Rules for Both Tracks No amendment during emergency. No amendment under either track may be proposed, voted upon, or take effect during a declared national emergency. The amendment process resumes when the emergency lapses or is terminated. For purposes of this Article, "declared national emergency" means only an emergency declared under Article III §5 with full congressional approval as required by Article III §5.4. A unilateral presidential emergency declaration that has not received congressional approval under Article III §5.4 does not constitute a declared national emergency for purposes of this prohibition, and does not suspend, delay, or bar the amendment process. No retaliatory amendments. No amendment may be designed to target a specific individual, party, or group for differential treatment. The Supreme Court shall reject any such amendment at pre clearance. Rescission. A state may rescind its ratification of a proposed amendment at any time before the amendment is certified as fully ratified. Expiration. A proposed amendment that fails to achieve full ratification within the time limits specified above expires and may not be re proposed in identical form for 4 years. Section 3 — The Unamendable Core 3.1 — Enumerated Unamendable Provisions The following provisions of this Constitution may not be amended, repealed, suspended, or superseded under any process, by any majority, at any time: 1. Universal suffrage. The right of adult citizens to vote in free, fair, and regular elections, as established in Article I. 2. Free and regular elections. The requirement that federal elections be held on the schedules established by this Constitution and that their results be binding, as established in Article I. Substance over label protection (applies to provisions 1 and 2). The unamendable protections of provisions 1 and 2 apply to the substance of the rights they protect, not merely to their labels or formal designations. Any amendment that redefines "federal election," "eligible voter," "vote," or "election result" in a manner that narrows the practical exercise of the rights protected by provisions 1 or 2 — regardless of whether the amendment purports to amend those provisions directly — is subject to pre clearance review under Section 4 as if it amended an enumerated unamendable provision. The Supreme Court shall look through the form of such an amendment to its practical effect on the franchise and on election integrity. 3. Peaceful transfer of power. The requirement that power transfer to the lawfully elected successor following any federal election, as established in Articles II and III. 4. Anti subversion. The prohibition on attempting to subvert a lawful election or constitutional transfer of power, and the penalties therefor, as established in Article VI. 5. Electoral Commission independence. The independence, mandate, and protection from political direction of the Electoral Commission, as established in Article XII. 6. Accountability Commission independence. The independence, mandate, and protection from political direction of the Accountability Commission, as established in Article XII. Both Constitutional Organs are equally essential to democratic integrity; the independence of one cannot be preserved while the other is dismantled. 7. Judicial independence. The protections against politically motivated removal of federal judges and the independence of the federal judiciary, as established in Article IV. 8. Separation of powers. The division of governmental authority among the Legislative, Executive, and Judicial branches such that no single branch may exercise the full powers of another. 9. Freedom of political expression. The freedoms of speech, press, and peaceful assembly as they apply to political activity, as established in Article V. 10. Due process and habeas corpus. The right to due process of law and the protection against arbitrary detention or imprisonment, as established in Article V. 11. This Section. Section 3 of this Article, in its entirety, may not be amended, repealed, or altered. 3.2 — Principled Backstop No amendment may permanently concentrate unchecked governmental power in a single person, political party, or institution. No amendment may eliminate or materially impair the capacity of citizens to alter their government through free and regular elections and lawful transfer of power. The Supreme Court shall enforce this Section against evasive or functionally equivalent changes as well as against amendments that directly contradict Section 3.1. An amendment materially impairs that capacity if, in substance or practical effect, it: (a) meaningfully narrows the electorate on arbitrary, partisan, hereditary, or discriminatory grounds; (b) prevents federal elections from remaining free, regular, and binding; (c) disables a meaningful avenue of peaceful political change; (d) strips a branch, electorate, or constitutionally independent institution of the minimum powers necessary to check entrenched rule; or (e) entrenches a person, party, or governing bloc against electoral replacement beyond ordinary constitutional accountability. Section 4 — Judicial Pre Clearance 4.1 — Mandatory Review Before any proposed amendment under Track 1 or Track 2 is submitted to states for ratification, it must be submitted to the Supreme Court for pre clearance review. Ratification votes may not begin until the Court has issued its ruling. 4.2 — Review Standard The Supreme Court shall review the proposed amendment solely to determine: (a) Whether it conflicts with any provision of Section 3.1 (enumerated unamendable provisions) (b) Whether it violates the principled backstop of Section 3.2 (c) Whether it is properly classified under Track 1 or Track 2 — the Court shall determine the correct track if there is a dispute, and may reclassify an amendment to the appropriate track (d) Whether it targets a specific individual, party, or group in violation of Section 2 The Court may not review the wisdom, desirability, or policy merits of a proposed amendment. Its jurisdiction at this stage is limited to the four questions above. 4.3 — Timeline For Track 1 structural amendments , the Supreme Court must issue its pre clearance ruling within 90 days of submission. For Track 2 rights amendments , the Court must issue its ruling within 60 days. If no ruling is issued within the applicable deadline, pre clearance is deemed granted and ratification may proceed. Any period during which the Court is demonstrably prevented from acting by executive interference does not count toward the deadline; the Clerk of the Court shall publicly certify any such period with specificity. 4.4 — Outcomes Cleared: Ratification may proceed under the applicable track. Rejected: The amendment may not proceed to ratification. When the Supreme Court rejects an amendment at pre clearance, it must publish a written opinion stating with specificity the constitutional ground of rejection. Congress may revise and resubmit after a 90 day waiting period. Reclassified: The amendment proceeds under the correct track as determined by the Court. 4.5 — Standing Any member of Congress, any state legislature, or the President may petition the Supreme Court for pre clearance review. Citizens may submit amicus briefs during the review period. Section 5 — Post Ratification Review 5.1 — Challenge Window Any ratified amendment may be challenged in federal court as violating Section 3 of this Article within 2 years of certification of ratification. 5.2 — Standing to Challenge Any member of Congress, the legislature of any state, the President, the Accountability Commission, or any citizen whose rights are directly impaired by the amendment has standing to bring a post ratification challenge. Any citizen also has standing to challenge an amendment alleged to violate Section 3, regardless of particularized injury beyond the impairment of the constitutional order. 5.3 — Adjudication Post ratification challenges are heard directly by the Supreme Court, which has original and exclusive jurisdiction. The Court must issue a ruling within 180 days of the challenge being filed. 5.4 — Remedy If the Supreme Court finds that a ratified amendment violates Section 3, the amendment is void in its entirety from the date of the Court's ruling. The Constitution is restored to its prior state. Congress may not re propose the voided amendment for 10 years. 5.5 — Finality After the 2 year challenge window closes, a ratified amendment is immune from challenge on Section 3 grounds and becomes a permanent part of this Constitution. This finality provision does not apply to amendments that themselves violate Section 3 — a void amendment is void regardless of when the violation is raised. Section 6 — Transition at Ratification 6.1 Ratification transition, carried forward law, supersession of prior constitutional provisions, and first cycle implementation are governed by Article XIX. 6.2 The permanent amendment rules of this Article apply subject to the first cycle limitations and harmonizing rules established in Article XIX.

docs.title: Article XII — Constitutional Organs
docs.summary: Establishes two independent Constitutional Organs: the Electoral Commission and the Accountability Commission. These are not branches of government. They do not make law, set policy, or govern. They enforce the rules and integrity of the system that the three branches operate within.
docs.search_text: Article XII — Constitutional Organs Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes two independent Constitutional Organs: the Electoral Commission and the Accountability Commission. These are not branches of government. They do not make law, set policy, or govern. They enforce the rules and integrity of the system that the three branches operate within. Constitutional Organs have: Independence from all three branches Defined, narrow mandates they cannot exceed Constitutional standing to act without political direction Full subjection to judicial review A legitimacy chain rooted in multi branch appointment, transparency, and limited scope Section 1 — General Principles 1.1 Constitutional Organs derive their authority from this Constitution and the laws passed under it. They do not possess inherent governmental power. They enforce — they do not create. 1.2 No Constitutional Organ may issue regulations, make binding policy, or direct the actions of any branch of government except as explicitly authorized by this Article. 1.3 All actions of a Constitutional Organ are subject to judicial review. 1.4 No officer of a Constitutional Organ may hold any other public office, engage in partisan political activity, or accept outside employment during their term. 1.5 All proceedings, findings, and expenditures of Constitutional Organs shall be published and publicly accessible, except where a court has authorized temporary confidentiality for an active investigation. Section 2 — The Electoral Commission 2.1 Mandate. The Electoral Commission administers, protects, and certifies all federal elections and democratic processes assigned by this Constitution. It also enforces campaign finance law in federal elections and the minimum campaign finance standards made applicable to the States by this Constitution. 2.2 Powers. The Electoral Commission may: (a) administer registration, balloting, counting, certification, recall, referendum, initiative, redistricting, and overseas voting functions assigned by this Constitution or by law consistent with it; (b) require, receive, verify, and publish campaign finance disclosures and related public records required by this Constitution or by law consistent with it; (c) investigate civil violations within its mandate, adjudicate non criminal campaign finance violations, impose civil penalties authorized by law, and refer criminal matters or willful constitutional violations to the Accountability Commission; (d) audit state campaign finance enforcement bodies for compliance with the constitutional minimum standards and, where this Constitution so provides, trigger or seek the further remedies established elsewhere in this Constitution; (e) issue administrative rules and technical standards strictly necessary to carry out duties expressly assigned by this Constitution or by law. 2.3 Limits. The Electoral Commission may not: (a) prosecute criminal offenses directly; (b) make final unreviewable determinations of candidate eligibility except where this Constitution expressly assigns it an administrative ballot access function subject to judicial review; (c) administer state elections except where a State requests such administration or this Constitution or a federal court expressly requires it; (d) direct the investigative or prosecutorial decisions of the Accountability Commission. 2.4 Public transparency and systems. The Electoral Commission shall maintain public, timely, and machine readable election and campaign finance records sufficient to make the constitutional rules of democratic participation and political money practically enforceable. Congress shall by law provide the specific technical and reporting systems necessary to carry out this duty. Failure to maintain constitutionally adequate systems is subject to judicial relief at the suit of an affected voter, candidate, or Constitutional Organ. 2.5 Composition. The Electoral Commission consists of 7 Commissioners serving staggered 12 year non renewable terms. No more than 2 Commissioners may be affiliated with the same political party. Each Commissioner must have at least 10 years of professional experience in election law, election administration, campaign finance law, public administration, constitutional law, or a closely related field. No Commissioner may have held partisan elected office or served as a paid campaign official within the 10 years preceding appointment. 2.6 Appointment and removal. Commissioners are appointed by joint resolution requiring approval of the President, a majority of the House, a majority of the Regional Assembly, and the Chief Justice. They may be removed only by impeachment and only for serious misconduct, incapacity, or persistent neglect of constitutional duty. 2.7 Quorum and continuity. A majority of the full membership constitutes a quorum. The Commission may act through divisions or delegated officers only as authorized by law or internal rules consistent with this Constitution, but certification of federal elections, formal findings of systematic state non compliance, and final civil enforcement orders require action attributable to the Commission itself. The Commission shall maintain continuity procedures sufficient to preserve certification, recordkeeping, and emergency election administration during vacancy, attack, disaster, or other severe disruption. 2.8 Vacancies. Vacancies are governed by Section 5.6 of this Article. Section 3 — The Accountability Commission 3.1 Mandate. The Accountability Commission independently investigates, audits, and prosecutes corruption, abuse of office, public integrity violations, and other matters that this Constitution expressly places within its jurisdiction. 3.2 Powers. The Accountability Commission may: (a) investigate and prosecute corruption, bribery, self dealing, obstruction, abuse of office, anti subversion offenses, and other public integrity violations assigned to it by this Constitution or by law consistent with it; (b) audit federal expenditures, agencies, and programs, including classified activity where this Constitution assigns that oversight role to the Commission; (c) enforce federal ethics, disclosure, conflict of interest, merit system, and anti retaliation rules to the extent assigned by this Constitution or by law consistent with it; (d) receive criminal or willful misconduct referrals from the Electoral Commission and other bodies authorized by this Constitution; (e) publish findings, require written agency responses to audit findings, and seek judicial enforcement or other constitutional remedies where its lawful orders or constitutional functions are obstructed. 3.3 Independence and limits. The Accountability Commission operates independently of the Department of Justice and may not be directed, limited, or terminated in a specific matter by the President or by any other official. It may not: (a) sentence criminal defendants; (b) remove officers directly except where this Constitution expressly gives a certification or referral consequence; (c) investigate protected speech, journalism, or other conduct protected by Article V unless the investigation concerns corruption, conspiracy, obstruction, or another unprotected offense within its lawful jurisdiction; (d) absorb the Electoral Commission's ordinary civil campaign finance enforcement role. 3.4 Timely action and continuity. Where this Constitution or a lawful referral requires the Accountability Commission to act within a specified period, it shall either act within that period or publish a specific written explanation for any delay unless this Constitution provides a stronger consequence. Congress shall by law provide the staffing, classified access, and auditing infrastructure necessary for the Commission to discharge its constitutional duties. Failure of ordinary staffing, vacancy, or internal deadlock does not suspend duties expressly imposed by this Constitution. 3.5 Composition. The Accountability Commission consists of 9 Commissioners serving staggered 15 year non renewable terms. No more than 3 Commissioners may be affiliated with the same political party. Each Commissioner must be either a licensed attorney with at least 10 years of experience in criminal law, public corruption, constitutional law, or government oversight, or a certified public accountant or auditor with at least 10 years of experience in federal financial auditing or forensic accounting. No Commissioner may have held partisan elected office or served as a paid campaign official within the 10 years preceding appointment. 3.6 Appointment and removal. Commissioners are appointed by joint resolution requiring approval of the President, a majority of the House, a majority of the Regional Assembly, and a majority of the Supreme Court. They may be removed only by impeachment and only for serious misconduct, incapacity, or persistent neglect of constitutional duty. 3.7 Quorum and continuity. A majority of the full membership constitutes a quorum. The Commission may delegate ordinary investigative or auditing tasks by law or internal rule, but formal criminal charges, constitutional certifications, final audit findings, and final ethics orders must remain attributable to the Commission or to officers lawfully acting under authority specifically delegated by it. The Commission shall maintain continuity procedures sufficient to preserve investigations, classified oversight, and time sensitive constitutional certifications during vacancy, attack, disaster, or other severe disruption. 3.8 Vacancies. Vacancies are governed by Section 5.6 of this Article. Section 4 — Citizen Access 4.1 — Right to petition. Any citizen may petition either Constitutional Organ to open an investigation or inquiry into a matter within that Organ's mandate. A petition must be signed by at least 500 citizens from at least 5 states and must describe with reasonable specificity the conduct alleged and the provision of this Constitution or federal law that the petitioner believes has been violated. Organizations with membership in at least 5 states may file a petition on behalf of their members without individual signature collection. 4.2 — Response required. Upon receiving a qualifying petition, the relevant Commission shall within 60 days either: (a) Open a formal inquiry and notify the petitioners (b) Decline and publish a written explanation of why the matter does not fall within its mandate or does not meet the threshold for action While the Commission's decision to decline a petition is not subject to judicial review on the merits, a citizen may challenge the decision if: (a) the Commission failed to respond within 60 days; (b) the Commission's written explanation does not address the specific conduct alleged; or (c) the same conduct was previously referred by a congressional committee. Courts may order the Commission to provide a compliant written explanation but may not order it to open an investigation. The decision to open or not open a specific investigation remains within the Commission's prosecutorial discretion. 4.3 — No anonymous petitions. Petitions submitted under this Section must include the verified identities of the signatories. The Commission shall maintain petitioner identity records confidentially and may not disclose them except where a petitioner is themselves a witness or party in a proceeding that results from the petition. 4.4 — Protection from retaliation. A citizen who submits a qualifying petition in good faith is protected from retaliation by any federal official or agency under Article VIII Section 3.4. Section 5 — Shared Protections 5.1 Funding independence. The budgets of both Constitutional Organs are set by law and may not be reduced below the prior year's level except by a 2/3 vote of both chambers. This protects against defunding as a form of political pressure. 5.2 No direction. No official of any branch may direct, order, or attempt to influence the investigative or administrative decisions of either Constitutional Organ. Attempting to do so is a criminal offense subject to prosecution by the Accountability Commission. 5.3 Whistleblower protection. Any person who reports a credible violation to either Constitutional Organ is protected from retaliation by any federal official or agency. Retaliation is itself a prosecutable offense. 5.4 Coordination. The two Commissions may share information and coordinate investigations where mandates overlap. They may not merge, consolidate, or delegate core functions to each other or to any other body. 5.5 Internal anti sabotage rule. No Commissioner may refuse quorum, certification, record publication, or other constitutionally required institutional action for the purpose of disabling the Organ's constitutional functions. Law may provide internal emergency procedures, temporary acting officers, and expedited judicial relief sufficient to preserve required institutional action when a Constitutional Organ is threatened by internal boycott or comparable bad faith paralysis. 5.6 Vacancies and temporary appointments. If a vacancy on either Constitutional Organ remains unfilled for more than 90 days because an appointing authority failed to act, the Chief Justice may make a temporary appointment from among persons meeting the qualification requirements of Sections 2 or 3. A temporary appointment lasts until the ordinary appointing process is completed or for 180 days, whichever comes first, and may be renewed once upon written findings by a majority of the Supreme Court that the appointing process remains deadlocked despite good faith efforts to complete it. Temporary Commissioners may exercise only the ordinary powers of the office and do not become permanent Commissioners except through the ordinary appointment process. Section 6 — Transition at Ratification 6.1 — Bootstrapping problem. The normal appointment mechanism for Constitutional Organ Commissioners requires the President, the House, the Regional Assembly, and the Supreme Court — none of which may be fully constituted under this Constitution's procedures at the moment of ratification. The following one time transitional process resolves this. 6.2 — Initial appointments. Within 180 days of ratification, the first Commissioners of both Constitutional Organs shall be appointed by the following transitional process: (a) The first Congress seated under this Constitution shall, within 90 days of convening, establish a joint transitional appointments committee consisting of equal numbers of members from the House and the Regional Assembly (b) This committee shall nominate candidates meeting the qualification requirements of Sections 2 and 3 of this Article (c) Initial appointments shall be confirmed by majority vote of both the House and Regional Assembly, voting separately (d) The Chief Justice of the Supreme Court shall certify the appointments as compliant with the qualification requirements 6.3 — Staggered initial terms. To establish the intended staggering, initial Commissioners shall be assigned terms of varying lengths by lot, so that no more than one third of each Commission's seats expire in any single four year period. Commissioners whose initial terms are shorter than the full term are eligible to be appointed to one full term thereafter, notwithstanding the non renewable term rule. 6.4 — This Section is transitional. Once both Commissions are fully constituted under this transitional process, Section 6 has no further operative effect. All subsequent appointments and vacancies are governed by Sections 2 and 3.

docs.title: Article XIII — Federal Agencies and the Administrative State
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional framework governing federal agencies, commissions, and administrative bodies created by Congress. The administrative state is the primary instrument through which the federal government implements law in practice. Its structure, independence, accountability, and integrity are matters of constitutional importance. This Article does not enumerate specific agencies — those are created, modified, and abolished by statute — but establishes the structural rules that all agencies must follow and the protections that certain categories of agency must receive. Where another Article imposes agency-specific substantive duties or additional safeguards, those provisions control in addition to this Article.

headings.text: Section 2 — Division of Authority Over Agencies
Section 4 — Protections for Independent Regulatory Agencies
Section 5 — Protection Against Abolition and Restructuring

docs.search_text: Article XIII — Federal Agencies and the Administrative State Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the constitutional framework governing federal agencies, commissions, and administrative bodies created by Congress. The administrative state is the primary instrument through which the federal government implements law in practice. Its structure, independence, accountability, and integrity are matters of constitutional importance. This Article does not enumerate specific agencies — those are created, modified, and abolished by statute — but establishes the structural rules that all agencies must follow and the protections that certain categories of agency must receive. Where another Article imposes agency specific substantive duties or additional safeguards, those provisions control in addition to this Article. Section 1 — Principles 1.1 Federal agencies exist to serve the public interest, not the interest of the industries they regulate, the officials who lead them, or the political party in power at any given time. 1.2 The legitimacy of the administrative state rests on three foundations: democratic accountability through Congress, which creates and funds agencies; executive accountability through the President, who directs agencies within the law; and structural independence for regulatory agencies whose effectiveness depends on freedom from political interference. 1.3 An agency captured by the industry it regulates, or directed by the executive to serve political rather than statutory purposes, has ceased to fulfill its constitutional function. This Article is designed to prevent both forms of capture. Section 2 — Division of Authority Over Agencies 2.0 — The fundamental division. Congress creates federal agencies; the President operates them. Congress defines what agencies must do; the President directs how they do it. Neither branch may use its authority over agencies to nullify the legitimate authority of the other. 2.0.1 — Congressional authority. Congress alone may: (a) Create, restructure, or abolish a federal agency (b) Define the agency's statutory mandate, jurisdiction, and powers (c) Appropriate funds for the agency's operations (d) Investigate the agency's conduct through oversight proceedings (e) Establish the internal structure, staffing rules, and procedural requirements of the agency by law 2.0.2 — Presidential authority. Within the scope of an agency's statutory mandate, the President may: (a) Appoint and, where permitted, remove agency leadership (b) Direct the priorities and policy emphasis of executive agencies (c) Issue lawful executive orders governing how agencies carry out their statutory duties (d) Coordinate agency activities through the Office of Management and Budget and other lawful executive mechanisms 2.0.3 — Duty to execute. The President shall faithfully execute all laws establishing federal agencies and defining their mandates. Directing an agency to refuse to carry out its statutory mandate, systematically declining to enforce a statute in order to nullify it in practice, redirecting an agency's resources to purposes outside its statutory mission, or using an agency's powers for purposes for which it was not created are violations of the President's duty under Article III and grounds for impeachment. The President's policy disagreement with a statute is not a lawful basis for non enforcement. 2.0.4 — Congressional non interference in operations. Congress may not direct the outcome of individual agency enforcement actions, adjudications, investigations, or regulatory proceedings. Congressional oversight authority is investigative and legislative in nature — it may examine whether the law is being followed, hold hearings, compel testimony, and legislate new requirements, but it may not instruct an agency on how to resolve a specific case or proceeding. Congress may not use appropriations riders to direct the outcome of pending enforcement actions against named parties. 2.0.5 — No weaponization. Neither the President nor Congress may direct any federal agency to: (a) Target individuals, organizations, or businesses for adverse action on the basis of their political beliefs, political affiliation, or support for or opposition to any candidate or party (b) Withhold regulatory approvals, licenses, contracts, or benefits as retaliation for political opposition (c) Conduct or support surveillance of domestic political activity for political rather than lawful law enforcement purposes Use of agency power for political retaliation is a violation of this Constitution and grounds for removal of the responsible official. Section 3 — Classification of Federal Agencies 3.1 — Executive agencies. An executive agency is one whose head serves at the pleasure of the President and whose activities are subject to direct presidential direction within the bounds of the agency's statutory mandate. The President may remove the head of an executive agency at any time and for any reason. Cabinet departments are executive agencies. 3.2 — Independent regulatory agencies. An independent regulatory agency is one whose commissioners or members serve fixed terms established by law and may be removed only for cause. Independent regulatory agencies are established where Congress determines that effective regulation requires structural insulation from direct presidential control — typically because the agency exercises quasi judicial or quasi legislative functions, or regulates an industry with significant political influence. 3.3 — Congress shall designate. When Congress creates a federal agency, it shall designate it as an executive agency or an independent regulatory agency. Ambiguity in the designation shall be resolved in favor of independent status where the agency exercises adjudicatory, prosecutorial, or financial regulatory functions. 3.4 — Constitutional Organs. The Electoral Commission and Accountability Commission established in Article XII are a separate category. They are Constitutional Organs, not agencies of either the executive or legislative branch. The provisions of this Article do not govern them except where this Article explicitly says otherwise. 3.5 — Article specific regulators. Congress may create regulators or commissions subject both to this Article and to additional requirements elsewhere in this Constitution. In such cases, this Article governs general agency structure unless another Article supplies a narrower or stricter rule. Section 4 — Protections for Independent Regulatory Agencies 4.1 — For cause removal only. A commissioner, member, or director of an independent regulatory agency may be removed by the President only upon a finding of: (a) Demonstrated misconduct in office (b) Neglect of duty (c) Incapacity to perform the duties of the position (d) Conviction of a felony Disagreement with an agency's regulatory decisions, enforcement choices, rulemaking priorities, or any exercise of lawful statutory authority is not cause for removal. The President may not remove a commissioner for making a decision the President dislikes. 4.2 — Removal process. The President shall provide written notice of the grounds for removal to the commissioner and to the relevant oversight committees of Congress. The commissioner shall have thirty days to respond. Removal may be challenged in federal court, and a timely challenge stays the removal pending judicial review unless the court finds immediate suspension necessary for misconduct or incapacity. 4.3 — No direction of individual cases. The President may not direct, delay, accelerate, or terminate any specific enforcement action, investigation, adjudication, or rulemaking proceeding of an independent regulatory agency. The President may communicate general policy priorities to agency leadership through lawful channels, but may not instruct an agency on the outcome of any specific matter. 4.4 — Appointment. The President shall appoint commissioners of independent regulatory agencies with the advice and consent of the Regional Assembly. Where a commission is designed to be bipartisan, no more than a bare majority of its members may be of the same political party. The President may not fill a commission with all members of one party where the governing statute requires balance. Section 5 — Protection Against Abolition and Restructuring 5.1 — No abolition by executive order. The President may not abolish, merge, reorganize, or transfer the statutory functions of a federal agency by executive order alone. An agency created by Congress may be abolished, merged, or substantially reorganized only by an act of Congress signed into law or enacted over a veto. 5.2 — No abolition by defunding. Congress may not effectively abolish an independent regulatory agency by reducing its appropriation below the level necessary to carry out its core statutory mandate, except by a 2/3 vote of both chambers accompanied by explicit findings that the agency's core functions are no longer necessary or are being transferred to another body. A budget reduction that renders an agency unable to operate constitutes a de facto abolition and is subject to the same requirements. 5.3 — Transfer of functions. The statutory functions of an independent regulatory agency may not be transferred to an executive agency without the consent of Congress by legislation. This prevents circumventing the independence of a regulatory body by reassigning its duties to a body under direct presidential control. 5.4 — Continuity during transition. When a new President takes office, all agency commissioners serving fixed terms shall continue in office until their terms expire or lawful cause for removal is established. A change in the presidency does not, by itself, constitute grounds for removing or suspending any commissioner of an independent regulatory agency. Section 6 — Accountability and Transparency 6.1 — Congressional oversight. Every federal agency shall be subject to oversight by the relevant committees of Congress. Agency heads shall appear before Congress when requested and shall provide requested documents and information, subject only to lawful claims of privilege adjudicated by a court. Blanket refusal to cooperate with congressional oversight is not permitted. 6.2 — Inspector General. Every major federal agency shall have an Inspector General appointed through a process designed to ensure independence from agency leadership. Inspectors General shall have access to agency records, personnel, and facilities necessary to detect and prevent waste, fraud, and abuse. No Inspector General may be removed without written notice to Congress specifying the reasons for removal. A removal that retaliates for an ongoing investigation is void. 6.3 — Public rulemaking. All rules of general applicability issued by federal agencies shall be subject to a notice and comment process during which any member of the public may submit written comments. The agency shall respond to significant comments before finalizing a rule. Emergency rules may take effect immediately but shall be subject to the full comment process retroactively. 6.4 — Judicial review. All final agency actions are subject to judicial review for compliance with the agency's authorizing statute and with this Constitution. Courts shall review agency legal interpretations independently. Courts shall defer to agency factual findings where supported by substantial evidence in the record. Courts shall not defer to agency interpretations of the scope of their own jurisdiction. 6.5 — Annual reporting. Every federal agency shall publish an annual public report sufficient to disclose its statutory mission, principal actions, use of funds, and significant capacity gaps without compromising ongoing investigations or protected information. Section 7 — Revolving Door: General Framework 7.1 — Principle. The revolving door between regulated industries and the agencies that regulate them corrupts regulatory independence and transfers public authority to private benefit. Every federal regulatory agency is subject to this Section. The specific provisions of Article VIII Section 2 governing the financial market regulator represent the constitutional floor for that regulator; this Section establishes the floor for all others. 7.2 — Post service cooling off. Senior officials of any federal regulatory agency may not, for a period set by Congress of not less than two years following their departure, work for, consult for, lobby, or accept compensation from any entity they directly regulated, investigated, or supervised during their service. Congress may establish longer cooling off periods tailored to particular agencies or offices, but may not set shorter ones. 7.3 — Transition support. Congress shall provide lawful transition support sufficient to ensure that mandatory cooling off periods do not function as a deterrent to qualified public service. 7.4 — Pre service restrictions. A person appointed to a senior regulatory position from a regulated industry shall be recused from all matters directly involving their former employer for a period set by Congress of not less than two years. Deferred compensation or unvested equity from a regulated entity that creates a continuing financial incentive to favor that entity shall be forfeited upon appointment or placed under conditions that remove the incentive. 7.5 — Compensation floor. Congress shall maintain compensation for senior regulatory personnel at levels sufficient to attract and retain qualified professionals. Where significant pay gaps between government and regulated industry are documented, Congress shall address them. Chronic underpayment that structurally forces experienced officials to seek industry employment is incompatible with effective regulation. 7.6 — Public reporting. Law shall provide public transparency sufficient to enforce the post service restrictions of this Section. Section 8 — Civil Service Protections 8.1 The civil service protections of Article VIII Section 3 apply to all federal agency employees occupying career positions. This Section cannot be circumvented by reclassifying career positions as political appointments in excess of the limits established by Congress under Article VIII Section 3.3. 8.2 — Mass removal prohibited.
