No President may remove, suspend, or constructively discharge a class of federal employees defined by agency, job function, or lack of political affiliation in the absence of individual cause, documented performance failure, or a lawfully enacted reduction in force. A reduction in force must be based on genuine operational necessity and carried out under procedures established by law. It may not be used as a pretext for political removal. 8.3 — Schedule F and equivalents prohibited. No executive order, regulation, or administrative action may reclassify a class of career civil servants as at will employees subject to removal without cause, or create a category of federal employment that strips civil servants of the protections required by Article VIII Section 3 and this Section. Any such reclassification is void. 8.4 — Whistleblower protections. Agency employees who report evidence of illegal conduct, waste, fraud, abuse, constitutional violations, or threats to public health or safety to the Accountability Commission, to congressional oversight committees, or through internal reporting mechanisms are protected from retaliation under Article VIII Section 3.4. This protection applies regardless of security classification, except that it does not protect the disclosure of lawfully classified information through unauthorized channels. Section 9 — Captured Agencies 9.1 — Definition. An agency is captured when it systematically acts to advance the interests of the industry or entities it regulates rather than the public interest it was created to serve. Capture may occur through personnel decisions, enforcement choices, rulemaking, or structural relationships between agency officials and regulated parties. 9.2 — Congressional response. Where the Accountability Commission or a congressional oversight committee documents a pattern of agency capture, Congress shall within two years either restructure the agency, reassign its functions, or enact legislation materially addressing the documented cause of capture. Failure to act does not excuse continued capture. 9.3 — Accountability Commission jurisdiction. The Accountability Commission has jurisdiction to investigate and prosecute agency officials who engage in regulatory corruption, defined as the use of official regulatory authority to benefit a regulated entity in exchange for anything of value, including future employment. Regulatory corruption is a federal crime regardless of whether the benefit is received during or after government service. Section 10 — Sunset and Reauthorization 10.1 Congress may subject any federal agency to a sunset and reauthorization requirement when the agency is created. A sunset provision shall specify the date on which the agency's statutory authority lapses if not reauthorized, and the process for reauthorization. 10.2 No agency shall be subjected to a sunset period of less than ten years. Shorter sunset periods are prohibited because they subject agencies to annual threats of elimination that can be used as leverage to influence regulatory decisions. 10.3 Where an executive agency is subject to a sunset requirement and Congress fails to reauthorize it by the sunset date, the agency's existing rules and regulations remain in effect for two years following the sunset date, during which period the agency's functions shall be carried out by the relevant Cabinet department under the direction of the President. Where an independent regulatory agency is subject to a sunset requirement and Congress fails to reauthorize it by the sunset date, the agency shall continue for two years in caretaker form under its existing independent leadership solely to preserve ongoing proceedings, administer existing law, and prevent regulatory collapse while Congress acts. In either case, existing rules may be changed only through lawful rulemaking. Section 11 — Limit on Political Appointments 11.1 — Purpose. Political appointments serve a legitimate function: they allow elected Presidents to place senior officials aligned with their policy direction in positions of executive leadership. But a civil service that is predominantly political appointees — subject to removal at will and hired for loyalty rather than competence — is not a professional civil service at all. This Section establishes a constitutional ceiling on the total number of political appointments across the executive branch. 11.2 — Cap on political appointees. The total number of political appointees across all executive agencies and departments, meaning positions not covered by civil service merit protections and filled at the pleasure of the President or a presidentially appointed official, shall not exceed one percent of the total number of civilian federal employees at any time. Officers removable only for cause are not political appointees for purposes of this cap. 11.3 — No reclassification as workaround. The cap of Section 11.2 applies to all positions subject to at will removal by political officials, regardless of title, category, or form of creation. A presidential action that reclassifies career positions as political appointments is void to the extent it would evade or exceed the constitutional cap. 11.4 — Public accounting. Law shall provide public accounting and independent review sufficient to enforce this Section. Congress shall establish by law the categories of political appointment, and the President may not create new categories beyond those established by law. Section 12 — Critical Infrastructure Continuity 12.1 Congress may establish continuity and integrity standards for elections infrastructure, power and energy systems, telecommunications networks, ports and logistics, payment systems, and public health supply chains where their failure or hostile control would materially impair the capacity of constitutional institutions to function or of citizens to exercise constitutional rights. Standards established under this Section are subject to public accountability, congressional oversight, and the judicial review provisions of Article IV. 12.2 Nothing in this Section authorizes open ended military control of civilian infrastructure. Emergency measures authorized under this Section are subject to the limitations on emergency powers in Article III, the civil liberties protections of Article V, and independent judicial review. The President may take emergency action under this Section only within authority expressly granted by statute, subject to congressional approval on the timeline required by Article III.

docs.title: Article XIV — Taxation and Public Revenue
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional principles governing taxation and public revenue. This Article concerns how public money is raised. Article XV governs how money is borrowed, appropriated, and spent.
docs.search_text: Article XIV — Taxation and Public Revenue Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the constitutional principles governing taxation and public revenue. This Article concerns how public money is raised. Article XV governs how money is borrowed, appropriated, and spent. Section 1 — General Principle 1.1 Public revenue may be raised only under law. 1.2 Taxation is an instrument of democratic self government and must remain publicly accountable, transparent, and subject to constitutional control. 1.3 No tax system may be structured or administered to create concealed tribute, arbitrary favoritism, or practical disenfranchisement inconsistent with this Constitution. Section 2 — Power to Tax 2.1 Congress shall have power to lay and collect taxes, duties, imposts, and excises for public purposes consistent with this Constitution. 2.2 Tax laws must be enacted by Congress and may not be created, repealed, or materially altered by executive action except as authorized by law. 2.3 Revenue measures must originate in the House of Representatives as provided elsewhere in this Constitution. 2.4 Congress may design progressive, proportional, or mixed systems of taxation, subject to the equal protection, due process, anti corruption, and anti discrimination constraints of this Constitution. 2.5 No tax may be imposed or structured as a bill of attainder, a disguised punishment directed at named persons, or a retaliatory instrument against protected speech, lawful political opposition, journalism, or disfavored minorities. 2.6 Public revenue measures must be read together with Article XV, which governs borrowing, appropriations, budget continuity, and expenditure. No revenue rule may be construed to authorize concealed finance, off budget evasion, or expenditure outside the law governed fiscal system established by that Article. Section 3 — Revenue Integrity 3.1 Congress may require public reporting, anti evasion rules, audit mechanisms, and enforcement systems sufficient to preserve the integrity of the revenue system. 3.2 No officer may collect, waive, redirect, or selectively withhold taxes except as authorized by law. 3.3 No tax exemption, subsidy, or comparable revenue preference may be used as a disguised means of corruption, partisan retaliation, or circumvention of constitutional limitations. 3.4 Congress shall provide by law for disclosure and regular review of tax expenditures, credits, exemptions, deductions, and comparable revenue preferences sufficient to preserve democratic accountability over the effective revenue system, subject to narrow privacy protections. Section 4 — Uniformity and Fair Administration 4.1 Congress shall provide by law for uniform and reviewable procedures for tax assessment, collection, challenge, and refund. 4.2 No person may be deprived of property through tax administration without due process of law. 4.3 Tax administration may not be weaponized against political opponents, dissenters, journalists, or disfavored groups. 4.4 Revenue laws may distinguish among persons, entities, transactions, and forms of property only on grounds publicly stated in law and consistent with equal protection, legitimate public purpose, and fair administration. 4.5 Congress may authorize emergency revenue measures only by law, for publicly stated purposes, and for limited duration. No emergency revenue measure may be continued indefinitely by executive action alone. 4.6 Law shall provide a simple and accessible means for persons to determine, confirm, contest, and discharge tax liability, including any lawful system of simplified or automatic assessment, withholding, and collection consistent with due process, equal treatment, and meaningful opportunity to correct error. 4.7 No person shall be penalized for failure caused principally by unreasonable administrative complexity, material reporting failure by the state or a legally required reporting party, or the state's refusal to make available a lawful means of determining and satisfying tax liability. Revenue administration must remain sufficiently transparent that the public can understand the effective structure of taxation, including major tax expenditures and comparable revenue preferences, subject to narrow privacy protections consistent with this Constitution. Section 5 — Construction 5.1 This Article shall be construed to preserve democratic control of public revenue, fair administration, and resistance to concealed extraction or partisan misuse. 5.2 No ambiguity in this Article shall be resolved in favor of executive tax nullification, discriminatory enforcement, or hidden systems of public finance.

docs.title: Article XV — Budget, Public Credit, and Appropriations
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional principles governing borrowing, appropriations, fiscal continuity, budget integrity, classified spending, and public credit. Article XIV governs how revenue is raised. This Article governs how public money is authorized, financed, disclosed, and spent.

headings.text: Section 3 — Appropriations and Continuity of Government
Section 7 — Long-Term Responsibility and Emergency Finance

docs.search_text: Article XV — Budget, Public Credit, and Appropriations Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the constitutional principles governing borrowing, appropriations, fiscal continuity, budget integrity, classified spending, and public credit. Article XIV governs how revenue is raised. This Article governs how public money is authorized, financed, disclosed, and spent. Section 1 — General Fiscal Principles 1.1 Public money may be borrowed, appropriated, and spent only under law. 1.2 Borrowing, appropriations, and expenditure are instruments of democratic self government and must remain publicly accountable, transparent, and subject to constitutional control. 1.3 No public financial system may be designed to conceal obligations, evade appropriations discipline, or frustrate lawful spending already enacted. Section 2 — Borrowing and Public Credit 2.1 Congress shall have power to borrow on the credit of the United States. 2.2 No separate legislative debt ceiling may be used to threaten default on obligations already lawfully incurred. 2.3 The public credit of the United States shall not be impaired by a refusal to honor obligations previously authorized by law. 2.4 Borrowing authority must remain subordinate to law. No executive officer may create, conceal, or materially restructure public obligations outside the borrowing authority granted by Congress. Section 3 — Appropriations and Continuity of Government 3.1 No money shall be drawn from the Treasury except pursuant to appropriations made by law. 3.2 Congress shall pass appropriations legislation for the coming fiscal year by September 30. If appropriations legislation has not been enacted by October 1, an automatic continuing resolution at 98 percent of the prior year's enacted funding levels takes effect and remains in force until appropriations legislation is enacted. The government may not shut down for lack of appropriations. 3.3 The automatic continuing resolution is self executing. It requires no further legislative or executive action to take effect, may not be reinterpreted or altered by executive action, and may not fund new programs, expand existing programs, or begin capital projects not already underway. 3.4 No officer may alter appropriated funding ratios, withhold lawfully appropriated funds, or selectively implement appropriations for partisan, personal, or unlawful reasons. Any such action is subject to immediate judicial review on an expedited basis, with standing held by any member of Congress and by any person or entity directly affected. 3.5 Revenue and appropriations measures must originate in the House of Representatives as provided in Article II, but the fiscal principles and continuity guarantees of this Article bind both chambers and every officer of the United States. Section 4 — Budget Integrity 4.1 All federal expenditures, liabilities, and material financial obligations must appear in the official federal budget or in constitutionally authorized disclosures connected to it. 4.2 Off budget devices, concealed liabilities, and functionally equivalent evasions are prohibited. 4.3 Congress may not appropriate funds in excess of projected revenues plus authorized borrowing except by a 3/5 vote of both chambers declaring a fiscal emergency. Such a declaration must state the specific reason, scale, and expected duration of the exceptional financing. 4.4 No appropriations bill may receive a final vote before the independent fiscal analysis body established by this Article has published its fiscal analysis, except in a constitutionally sufficient fiscal emergency defined by law. Any emergency exception must be publicly certified under standards established by law and may not be used to evade ordinary review for convenience or partisan advantage. 4.5 Congress may by law require long term fiscal projections and program performance reporting sufficient to support informed budgeting and democratic accountability. 4.6 No classification label, emergency designation, or accounting device may be used to evade the disclosure, audit, or law governed spending requirements of this Constitution. Section 5 — Transparency and Public Reporting 5.1 The federal budget shall be published in a public, searchable, and intelligible form. 5.2 Citizens have a right to know the aggregate scale of public spending, public debt, and classified spending, subject only to narrow security exceptions consistent with this Constitution. 5.3 Congress may by law require public fiscal summaries, long term projections, and program performance reports sufficient to permit democratic oversight. 5.4 Congress shall maintain an independent fiscal analysis body with access sufficient to evaluate the budgetary and debt effects of major legislation and emergency finance proposals. Its methods and assumptions shall be public unless classification is strictly necessary to protect national security. Section 6 — Classified and Exceptional Spending 6.1 Classified spending is an exception to ordinary transparency, not a separate fiscal order exempt from constitutional accountability. 6.2 No classified spending program may be concealed from the congressional committees and oversight structures designated by law to supervise it. A program whose existence or budget cannot be disclosed to the constitutionally authorized oversight process may not be funded. 6.3 The Accountability Commission shall maintain an appropriately cleared audit function to review classified spending for legality, authorized purpose, and material waste or fraud, and shall report its findings to Congress in classified form and to the public in unclassified summary form to the maximum extent consistent with national security. 6.4 The total dollar amount appropriated for classified programs in each fiscal year shall be publicly disclosed, even if individual program line items remain classified. 6.5 Classified appropriations may not be used for domestic political manipulation, unlawful surveillance, influence operations targeting domestic political actors on the basis of protected political activity, or evasion of ordinary constitutional limits. 6.6 No classified spending program may operate for more than five years without explicit reauthorization by Congress following review of necessity, legality, and compliance with its authorized scope. A program not reauthorized within that period lapses automatically. 6.7 Classified programs shall undergo periodic declassification review under standards established by law. Continued classification must be justified anew at each review; prior classification alone is insufficient. 6.8 No person shall be prosecuted, penalized, or retaliated against for disclosing through lawful secure channels to Congress, the Accountability Commission, or a federal court evidence that a classified program is operating illegally, unconstitutionally, or in violation of this Article. Congress shall establish secure channels for such disclosures by law. 6.9 Any officer who knowingly conceals classified spending from the constitutionally authorized oversight process, or who spends classified funds for an unlawful domestic political purpose, is subject to removal, disqualification, and prosecution as provided elsewhere in this Constitution. Section 7 — Long Term Responsibility and Emergency Finance 7.1 Congress may authorize deficit spending and emergency finance where necessary, but only under public law and with procedures sufficient to identify the reason, scale, and expected duration of such measures. 7.2 The Constitution does not require balanced budgets in all circumstances, but it does require that borrowing and spending remain law governed, transparent, and reviewable. 7.3 Fiscal emergencies may justify temporary departures from ordinary procedures only on terms established by law and consistent with this Constitution. 7.4 No fiscal emergency may be used to suspend elections, rights, judicial review, ordinary legislative oversight, or the requirement that public money be raised and spent under law. Section 8 — Construction 8.1 This Article shall be construed to preserve continuity of lawful government, transparency in public obligations, and resistance to fiscal extortion or concealment. 8.2 No ambiguity in this Article shall be resolved in favor of shutdown tactics, off budget evasions, or executive manipulation of funds lawfully appropriated by Congress.

docs.title: Article XVI — War Powers and National Security
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional framework for the use of military force and the conduct of war. The war-making power belongs to Congress. The President directs authorized operations and responds to immediate threats. This Article governs armed conflict, combat operations, and other uses of military force.

headings.text: Section 1 — Commander in Chief and War Powers Authority
Section 15 — Technology and Supply Chain Security

docs.search_text: Article XVI — War Powers and National Security Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the constitutional framework for the use of military force and the conduct of war. The war making power belongs to Congress. The President directs authorized operations and responds to immediate threats. This Article governs armed conflict, combat operations, and other uses of military force. Economic security, foreign influence defense, critical infrastructure protection, foreign intelligence collection, gray zone response short of sustained armed conflict, alliance reliability, and technology and supply chain security are established in Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security . Section 1 — Commander in Chief and War Powers Authority 1.1 — Commander in Chief. The President is commander in chief of the armed forces of the United States and of the state National Guards when called into federal service. This authority is exercised within the limits established by this Constitution. The power to initiate war belongs to Congress. The President's role as commander in chief is the authority to direct and conduct military operations once authorized — it is not independent authority to begin them. 1.2 — Congressional Authorization Required. The President may not commit the armed forces, cyber forces, intelligence personnel acting in a paramilitary capacity, or private military contractors in a combat role to any military operation abroad — offensive or defensive beyond immediate response — without either: (a) A formal declaration of war against a named state or state actor, passed by majority vote of both chambers of Congress; or (b) An Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF) meeting the requirements of Section 1.6 The distinction between "offensive" and "defensive" does not determine whether authorization is required. The duration, scale, and nature of the operation determine whether it constitutes a commitment of forces requiring authorization. A long running operation that began defensively requires authorization once it extends beyond the 30 day window of Section 1.5. 1.3 — Definition of Military Force. For purposes of this Article, "use of military force" includes: (a) Deployment of armed forces in a combat or combat support role in a foreign country (b) Offensive cyber operations — defined as operations designed to cause physical destruction, significant disruption to critical infrastructure, casualty producing effects, or the degradation of a foreign nation's military or governmental capacity (c) Lethal drone or aerial strikes against targets in a foreign country (d) Deployment of private military contractors in a combat role or in direct support of combat operations (e) Covert paramilitary operations conducted by intelligence agencies involving lethal force against foreign nationals (f) Naval blockades or maritime operations designed to coerce a foreign government Defensive cyber operations, intelligence collection operations that do not involve lethal force, sanctions, diplomatic action, and other non military countermeasures governed by Article XVII are not "use of military force" for purposes of this Article. 1.4 — Defensive Action Without Prior Authorization. The President may, without prior congressional authorization, order military action to: (a) Repel an attack on United States territory, United States armed forces, or treaty allies under active attack pursuant to Section 6 (b) Protect United States military forces under imminent and specific threat (c) Execute a time critical operation to rescue United States citizens in immediate physical danger (d) Respond to an offensive cyber attack against United States critical infrastructure by neutralizing the attacking capability, limited to disabling the specific systems, tools, or infrastructure used in the attack in progress Defensive action under this Section must be proportionate to the threat, limited in scope to the specific defensive objective, and reported to Congress under Section 1.5. It does not authorize a broader military campaign, occupation of foreign territory, or sustained hostilities. 1.5 — Reporting, Authorization, and Enforcement. (a) Reporting. Within 24 hours of ordering military action under Section 1.4 or any other use of military force, the President shall submit a written report to Congress describing the action, its legal basis, its anticipated duration, and the forces involved. (b) Authorization window. Military action taken under Section 1.4 must receive congressional authorization within 30 days or forces must be withdrawn. Failure of Congress to authorize within that period has the same legal effect as a vote against authorization and constitutes nonauthorization for every purpose of this Article. (c) Enforcement — withdrawal order. If authorization is not granted within 30 days, the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court, or the judicial officer designated under Article IV Section 2.7, shall issue a binding withdrawal order enforceable by the federal courts. The Secretary of Defense shall ensure compliance within the period fixed by law, which may not exceed 15 days. Presidential defiance of a withdrawal order is a grave constitutional violation and an absolute no immunity category under Article III Section 15. (d) Continued operations after withdrawal order. Ordering or maintaining military operations in violation of a withdrawal order is a violation of this Constitution, grounds for impeachment, a criminal abuse of official power, and an unlawful order under Section 8. Upon issuance of the withdrawal order, no funds may be obligated or expended for continued unlawful operations except as strictly necessary to protect forces during disengagement. If the President purports to continue the operation after the withdrawal order takes effect, lawful operational authority is limited to terminating, withdrawing, or defending withdrawing forces; no officer is required or permitted to continue the unlawful operation on the President's command. Upon the President's defiance of a withdrawal order, the Accountability Commission shall immediately issue a fiscal hold on appropriated funds for the unlawful operation. The hold is self executing and does not require congressional authorization or executive cooperation. The hold does not apply to expenditures strictly necessary to protect forces during withdrawal and disengagement, as preserved by the prior sentence; Congress shall by law establish the process by which the Accountability Commission identifies and designates such expenditures promptly upon issuing a hold. The Secretary of the Treasury shall implement the hold within 24 hours of the Commission's order. A Treasury officer who fails to implement a lawful fiscal hold within that period is subject to immediate removal from office and referral for prosecution by the Accountability Commission. The fiscal hold may be lifted only by the Chief Justice, or the judicial officer designated under Article IV Section 2.7, upon certification that the unlawful operation has ended and forces have been or are actively being withdrawn. (e) Expedited impeachment vote. A presidential order maintaining operations in violation of a withdrawal order shall trigger the expedited impeachment timetable of Article III Section 10.2A. (f) No self authorization. Only an act of Congress constitutes authorization under this Article. 1.6 — Requirements for an Authorization for Use of Military Force. An Authorization for Use of Military Force (AUMF) passed by Congress must: (a) Name the enemy specifically; open ended references to associated or future forces are void (b) State the objective of the authorization (c) Define the geographic scope (d) Define the categories of force authorized (e) Expire not more than two years after passage unless renewed by Congress (f) Require periodic reporting to Congress An AUMF that does not meet these requirements is void. Congress may not delegate open ended war making authority to the President through vague or unlimited authorizations. 1.7 — Sunset and Renewal of Existing AUMFs. All Authorizations for Use of Military Force enacted before ratification are void one year after ratification unless renewed in conformity with Section 1.6. The President shall report to Congress within 90 days on all operations then being conducted under pre existing authorizations. Section 2 — Cyber Warfare 2.1 — Offensive cyber operations require authorization. Offensive cyber operations as defined in Section 1.3(b) require the same congressional authorization as conventional military force. The President may not order an offensive cyber operation against a foreign nation or actor without a declaration of war, an AUMF covering that operation, or defensive authorization under Section 1.4(d). 2.2 — Proportionality. Offensive cyber operations must be proportionate to the threat or objective. Cyber operations designed to cause civilian casualties, destroy civilian infrastructure unrelated to military objectives, or produce effects equivalent to a conventional military attack require authorization commensurate with that level of force. Proportionality under this Section shall be judged by the scale of harm, the degree of civilian risk, and the relationship of the response to the triggering attack. 2.3 — Disclosure to Congress. All offensive cyber operations conducted under any authorization shall be disclosed to the relevant congressional oversight committees within 48 hours of execution. Classification of the specific operational details is permitted; the existence and general nature of the operation may not be withheld from oversight committees. 2.4 — Defensive cyber operations. The President may order defensive cyber operations — including active defense measures that operate on foreign systems to neutralize an ongoing attack — without prior congressional authorization. Defensive cyber operations must be reported to Congress within 24 hours under Section 1.5(a). Section 3 — Nuclear Weapons 3.1 — No unilateral first use. The President may not order the first use of nuclear weapons without confirmation by the Secretary of Defense and the Secretary of State that the order is lawful and warranted, a written finding by the Director of National Intelligence that no non nuclear response is sufficient, and immediate notification to congressional leadership. If either Secretary declines to confirm the order, it does not proceed. 3.2 — Congressional objection to first use. A first use order may not proceed if the Speaker of the House or the Majority Leader of the Regional Assembly formally objects, unless Congress overrides the objection by a 2/3 vote of both chambers. These requirements do not apply to a response to a confirmed nuclear attack governed by Section 3.3. 3.3 — Response to confirmed nuclear attack. If the United States or a treaty ally is under confirmed nuclear attack, the President may order a nuclear response without the confirmation requirement of Section 3.1. The response must be proportionate to the attack. Congressional leadership shall be notified as rapidly as the operational circumstances permit. 3.4 — Launch authority delegation. The President may not permanently or preemptively delegate nuclear launch authority to any other person. Temporary delegation during presidential incapacity follows the succession provisions of Article III Section 12. 3.5 — No autonomous launch. No automated system, artificial intelligence, or algorithm may be authorized to launch nuclear weapons without a direct human order from an authorized person. Autonomous or semi autonomous nuclear launch systems are prohibited. Section 4 — Private Military Contractors 4.1 — Authorization required. Private military contractors operating in a combat role or in direct support of combat operations abroad are subject to all war powers requirements of this Article. Their deployment must be reported to Congress under Section 1.5(a) as part of any military operation. They may not be used to circumvent the authorization requirements of Section 1.2. 4.2 — Laws of war. Private military contractors deployed by or on behalf of the United States government are subject to the laws of war and the Uniform Code of Military Justice. Violations are prosecutable in federal courts and by the Accountability Commission. 4.3 — Annual disclosure. The President shall report to Congress annually the number, locations, operational roles, and total cost of private military contractors deployed abroad. Operational details may be classified as necessary, but the existence, general location, mission category, and total cost of such deployments shall always be disclosed to the relevant congressional oversight committees. 4.4 — No substitution. Private military contractors may not be used as a substitute for authorized military force to avoid reporting requirements, to conduct operations in countries where military deployment would require authorization, or to conceal the scale of United States military engagement in a conflict. Section 5 — Covert Lethal Operations 5.1 — Authorization required. Covert operations conducted by intelligence agencies or special operations forces involving lethal force against foreign nationals require either: (a) An existing AUMF that covers the target, the territory, and the category of force; or (b) A lawful defensive justification under Section 1.4 together with prompt notification to the congressional intelligence leadership 5.2 — No operations against US citizens without warrant. No covert lethal operation may target a United States citizen anywhere in the world without a judicial warrant issued upon probable cause that the citizen poses an imminent and specific threat to the United States and that capture is not feasible. In exigent circumstances, a warrant application must be submitted within 72 hours after the operation. The warrant application and the court's ruling shall be made public within a period set by law unless a court makes a renewed finding that disclosure would cause specific and articulable harm to national security. 5.3 — Accountability Commission oversight. The Accountability Commission (Article XII) shall maintain a classified division with appropriate security clearances to audit covert lethal operations for compliance with this Section. The division shall report annually to the relevant congressional oversight committees in classified form and publish an unclassified summary. Section 6 — Treaty Based Defense Obligations A mutual defense treaty ratified by the Regional Assembly under Article III Section 9.2 constitutes advance congressional authorization for strictly defensive military action required to fulfill that specific treaty obligation. This authorization is limited to: (a) Defensive operations to repel an attack on the treaty ally (b) Operations specifically required by the terms of the ratified treaty (c) The scope and duration necessary to fulfill the immediate defensive obligation Any military action beyond strict treaty based defense — including offensive operations, operations in territories not covered by the treaty, or operations that extend beyond repelling the specific attack — requires separate congressional authorization under Section 1.2. The existence of a treaty obligation does not authorize an open ended military campaign. Section 7 — Domestic Deployment The armed forces, National Guard units in federal service, and all paramilitary forces under federal authority may not be deployed against civilians on domestic soil except: (a) To suppress an armed rebellion upon a declaration by Congress identifying the specific rebellion and authorizing specific military action (b) To provide disaster relief and humanitarian assistance, at the request of a state governor or by congressional authorization, in a non combat capacity (c) To protect specific federal facilities, personnel, or infrastructure against imminent physical attack, limited in scope to the specific protective mission For purposes of this Section, "armed rebellion" means the organized use of deadly force to overthrow government, seize and hold territory, or prevent federal law enforcement from operating in a defined area. Political protest, civil disobedience, and uncoordinated riots do not constitute armed rebellion under this Section. A presidential declaration of armed rebellion must be promptly reviewed by the Accountability Commission; if the Commission rejects the declaration, the deployment authority lapses. The President may not declare a domestic insurrection or rebellion for the purpose of deploying military force against peaceful protest, civil unrest, or political opposition. A deployment of armed forces against domestic civilians that is not authorized by this Section is void, and members of the armed forces are not required to carry it out under Section 8. Section 8 — Unlawful Orders No member of the armed forces, intelligence service, or any paramilitary force under federal authority may be ordered to carry out, and no such person is required to obey, a command that: (a) Violates this Constitution (b) Violates a law of the United States (c) Violates the laws of war as recognized under international treaty ratified by the United States (d) Orders the use of nuclear weapons in violation of Section 3 (e) Orders continued military operations in violation of a withdrawal order issued under Section 1.5(c) (f) Orders the deployment of armed forces against domestic civilians in violation of Section 7 A member of the armed forces who refuses to carry out an unlawful order under this Section is protected from punishment for that refusal. A superior who punishes a subordinate for refusing an unlawful order is subject to prosecution by the Accountability Commission. A member of the armed forces who carries out an unlawful order is not relieved of personal liability for the consequences of that order. Section 9 — Economic Security and Countermeasures Economic security and non military countermeasures are governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security . Nothing in this Article enlarges military power by implication from those authorities. Section 10 — Foreign Influence Operations Foreign influence operations are governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security and by Article VI where the conduct targets democratic processes directly. Section 11 — Critical Infrastructure Protection Critical infrastructure protection, outside the use of military force governed by this Article, is governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security . Section 12 — Foreign Intelligence Collection Foreign intelligence collection is governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security , subject to the limits on covert lethal operations established in Section 5 of this Article. Section 13 — Gray Zone Response Authority Gray zone response short of military force is governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security . Measures that constitute military force remain subject to this Article. Section 14 — Alliance Reliability Alliance reliability, treaty execution duties, and non war alliance questions are governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security , except where this Article governs the actual use of military force. Section 15 — Technology and Supply Chain Security Technology and supply chain security are governed by Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security .

docs.title: Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional framework for economic security, foreign influence defense, critical infrastructure protection, foreign intelligence collection, gray-zone response authority, alliance obligations, and technology security. These provisions address modern threats that fall below or alongside conventional military conflict, including economic coercion, information warfare, cyber attacks on civilian systems, and supply-chain vulnerabilities. Article XVI remains the exclusive framework for war, sustained hostilities, and other uses of military force.

headings.text: Section 7 — Technology and Supply Chain Security

docs.search_text: Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes the constitutional framework for economic security, foreign influence defense, critical infrastructure protection, foreign intelligence collection, gray zone response authority, alliance obligations, and technology security. These provisions address modern threats that fall below or alongside conventional military conflict, including economic coercion, information warfare, cyber attacks on civilian systems, and supply chain vulnerabilities. Article XVI remains the exclusive framework for war, sustained hostilities, and other uses of military force. Section 1 — Economic Security and Countermeasures 1.1 — Presidential authority. The President may, in response to a foreign nation's or actor's actions that constitute a threat to the national security or economic security of the United States, impose the following economic countermeasures without prior congressional authorization: (a) Targeted sanctions against named foreign persons, entities, and government officials (b) Asset freezes against foreign government controlled entities operating in the United States (c) Restrictions on specific categories of exports to identified foreign adversaries (d) Emergency restrictions on foreign investment in critical sectors 1.2 — Congressional review. Economic countermeasures imposed under Section 1.1 shall be reported to Congress within 48 hours. Congress may by majority vote of both chambers terminate any countermeasure within 60 days of imposition. Countermeasures not terminated remain in effect until the President lifts them or Congress acts otherwise. 1.3 — National Security Investment Review. Congress shall establish a permanent National Security Investment Review body to screen foreign acquisitions of United States businesses, real property, and assets in sectors critical to national security. The Review body shall operate independently of political direction. Its decisions to block or condition acquisitions shall be based on objective national security criteria established by law. Any presidential override of a blocking decision must be made in writing, transmitted promptly to Congress, and remains subject to nullification by law. 1.4 — Critical sectors defined. Congress shall define by law the sectors subject to investment review under Section 1.3. At minimum, the following shall be designated critical: defense and aerospace; semiconductors and advanced computing; telecommunications infrastructure; artificial intelligence; biotechnology and pharmaceuticals; energy production and distribution; water systems; and financial market infrastructure. 1.5 — Economic coercion as national security threat. Systematic foreign government efforts to create economic dependence — including through state subsidized market manipulation, predatory investment, and infrastructure financing designed to establish leverage — shall be recognized as a national security threat. The President shall report to Congress annually on identified economic coercion campaigns and the countermeasures taken or proposed. Section 2 — Foreign Influence Operations 2.1 — Scope. Foreign adversary influence operations extend beyond election interference to include ongoing campaigns to shape domestic political opinion, policy, and institutions through covert or undisclosed foreign government direction. This Section addresses those operations without limiting protected speech or legitimate foreign engagement. 2.2 — Mandatory disclosure of foreign government funding. Any organization operating within the United States — including nonprofit organizations, think tanks, academic institutions, media organizations, cultural programs, and advocacy groups — that receives funding from a foreign government or a foreign government controlled entity shall register with the Accountability Commission and publicly disclose: (a) The identity of the foreign government or entity providing funds (b) The amount received, updated quarterly (c) The purposes for which the funds are used This requirement applies regardless of whether the organization engages in political activity. Disclosure is required for any foreign government funding above a threshold set by Congress. The constitutional floor for foreign government funding registration is $10,000 per year; Congress may set lower thresholds but not higher. Non disclosure is a criminal offense. The Accountability Commission is the enforcement authority. 2.3 — State sponsored foreign media. Any media organization that is editorially directed, substantially funded, or operationally controlled by a foreign government shall be registered as a foreign government media outlet with the Accountability Commission. All content produced by a registered foreign government media outlet distributed within the United States shall be labeled as such. Registration does not prohibit distribution — it requires transparency. Failure to register is a criminal offense. In addition to mandatory registration, state sponsored media must display a prominent, clear, and standardized disclosure label on all content; the label must identify the sponsoring government and the relationship between the media outlet and that government; platforms that host state sponsored media are required to enforce the labeling requirement. 2.4 — Academic and research institutions. Academic and research institutions receiving foreign government funding above a threshold set by Congress for research programs, academic chairs, or institutional programs shall disclose that funding publicly. Institutions may not accept foreign government funding subject to conditions that restrict research conclusions, limit publication, or require access to research results by the foreign government. Accepting such conditions voids the institution's eligibility for federal funding. 2.5 — Enforcement. The Accountability Commission shall maintain a public registry of all organizations registered under this Section. The Commission shall investigate suspected undisclosed foreign government funding operations and refer criminal violations for prosecution. The Commission shall publish an annual report on identified foreign influence operations and their sponsors. Section 3 — Critical Infrastructure Protection 3.1 — Federal responsibility. The federal government bears primary responsibility for protecting critical infrastructure — as defined in Section 1.4 of this Article — from attack, sabotage, and degradation by foreign governments, foreign controlled entities, and terrorist organizations. This responsibility cannot be fully delegated to private owners of critical infrastructure. 3.2 — Minimum security standards. Congress shall establish by law minimum security standards for all critical infrastructure, whether publicly or privately owned. The standards shall address physical security, cybersecurity, supply chain integrity, and resilience to attack or disruption. Owners and operators of critical infrastructure shall comply with these standards as a condition of operating in the United States. Congress shall review and update these standards not less than every five years. 3.3 — Federal assessment authority. The executive branch shall conduct regular security assessments of all designated critical infrastructure. Owners and operators of critical infrastructure shall cooperate with federal security assessments. The discovery of known foreign government implanted malware, surveillance tools, or access mechanisms in critical infrastructure systems shall be reported promptly to Congress. 3.4 — Foreign ownership of critical infrastructure prohibited. No foreign government, foreign government controlled entity, or entity with a foreign government beneficial owner holding more than a threshold set by Congress may own, operate, or hold a controlling interest in any designated critical infrastructure within the United States. Existing holdings in violation of this Section shall be divested within a period set by law that may not exceed two years after ratification or designation. 3.5 — Attack on critical infrastructure as act of war. A foreign government directed attack on United States critical infrastructure that causes significant physical damage, significant disruption to essential services, or loss of life shall be treated as an act of war for purposes of Article XVI. Section 4 — Foreign Intelligence Collection 4.1 — Purpose and balance. The collection of foreign intelligence — information about the activities, intentions, and capabilities of foreign governments, foreign organizations, and foreign nationals — is a necessary function of national security. This Section establishes a constitutional framework for foreign intelligence collection that preserves civil liberties while enabling effective defense against foreign threats. 4.2 — Foreign Intelligence Court. Congress shall establish a specialized Foreign Intelligence Court composed of federal judges serving renewable three year assignments, drawn from the Article IV judiciary. The Court shall: (a) Issue warrants for the collection of foreign intelligence within the United States (b) Review the legality and scope of intelligence collection programs (c) Publish annual statistics on warrants issued, denied, and modified, in unclassified form (d) Appoint independent counsel to argue against government warrant applications in significant cases 4.3 — Warrant requirements for collection. Collection of foreign intelligence that involves the interception of communications, the search of premises, or the collection of data about specific identified persons within the United States requires a warrant from the Foreign Intelligence Court upon a showing of: (a) Probable cause that the target is an agent of a foreign power or a foreign intelligence service (b) That the information sought is foreign intelligence information (c) That the collection will be conducted using minimization procedures that limit the retention and dissemination of information about United States persons "Agent of a foreign power" means a person who (a) acts at the direction or control of a foreign government or intelligence service, (b) engages in clandestine intelligence activities on behalf of a foreign power, or (c) knowingly engages in sabotage, terrorism, or the unauthorized gathering of defense information for a foreign power; journalists, academics, and advocates engaged in lawful activity do not qualify regardless of foreign contact. 4.4 — United States persons — heightened protection. Collection of foreign intelligence that targets a United States citizen or permanent resident requires a higher showing: probable cause that the person is knowingly engaged in clandestine intelligence activities, sabotage, or terrorism on behalf of a foreign power. Incidental collection of United States persons' communications during foreign targeted collection shall be minimized; communications of United States persons may not be searched without a separate warrant under Section 4.3. 4.5 — Bulk collection limitations. Bulk collection of communications is permitted only for foreign to foreign communications transiting United States infrastructure. Bulk collection of domestic communications, or of communications where one end is a United States person, is prohibited without individual warrants under Section 4.3. 4.6 — Congressional oversight. All foreign intelligence collection programs shall be disclosed to the relevant congressional oversight committees. No collection program whose existence is concealed from Congress is lawful. The Accountability Commission's classified oversight division under Article XII Section 3 shall audit compliance with this Section annually. 4.7 — No domestic political use. Foreign intelligence collection authorities may not be used to collect information on domestic political organizations, candidates, elected officials, journalists, or citizens on the basis of their political views or associations. Use of foreign intelligence authorities for domestic political surveillance is a criminal offense prosecutable by the Accountability Commission without a statute of limitations. Section 5 — Gray Zone Response Authority 5.1 — Definition. Gray zone operations are hostile actions by foreign states or state directed actors that fall below the threshold of conventional military attack but are designed to harm United States interests, undermine democratic institutions, or coerce United States policy. They include: state sponsored disinformation campaigns targeting domestic political processes; foreign government directed sabotage of infrastructure; economic coercion through market manipulation or weaponized financial leverage; and hybrid operations combining cyber, information, and proxy forces. 5.2 — Presidential response authority. The President may respond proportionately to identified gray zone operations without triggering Article XVI only where the response itself does not constitute a use of military force: (a) The President must determine in writing that the operation constitutes a gray zone attack by an identified foreign state or state directed actor (b) The response must be proportionate to the nature and scale of the gray zone operation (c) Congressional notification must occur before gray zone operations begin unless delay would cause immediate and serious harm, in which case notification must occur promptly thereafter 5.3 — Scope of gray zone response. Permissible responses to gray zone operations include counter disinformation measures, economic countermeasures under Section 1, cyber responses that do not amount to military force under Article XVI, diplomatic measures, and targeted sanctions. Gray zone authority does not authorize sustained hostilities, territorial occupation, lethal operations beyond Article XVI, or any other measure that constitutes military force. 5.4 — Attribution standard. A gray zone determination under Section 5.2 requires a written intelligence assessment attributing the operation to a foreign state or state directed actor with high confidence. The determination must be reviewed by the Director of National Intelligence and the Attorney General and provided to the congressional intelligence committees. Section 6 — Alliance Reliability 6.1 — Treaty commitments are binding. The President shall faithfully execute all mutual defense and collective security treaty obligations ratified by the Regional Assembly. The President may not selectively honor, condition, or withhold treaty obligations based on personal, political, financial, or extraneous considerations. 6.2 — No conditioning of alliance obligations. A President who conditions the fulfillment of a treaty alliance commitment — including military assistance, intelligence sharing, or collective defense obligations — on any consideration other than the treaty's terms and the requirements of this Constitution violates the oath of office and is subject to prosecution by the Accountability Commission under Article VI Section 1 and to impeachment under Article III Section 10. 6.3 — Congressional affirmation. Either chamber of Congress may by resolution independently affirm the United States' commitment to any treaty alliance. Such a resolution does not bind the President to take specific military action but creates a public record of congressional intent and may be considered in any proceeding under Section 6.2. 6.4 — Withdrawal from collective security treaties. The President may not withdraw the United States from any mutual defense or collective security treaty without the approval of a 2/3 vote of the Regional Assembly. The 2/3 requirement for collective security treaties is higher than the majority required for other treaty withdrawal under Article III Section 9.4, reflecting the particular destabilizing effect of withdrawing from collective defense arrangements. Section 7 — Technology and Supply Chain Security 7.1 — Strategic technology protection. The federal government has a responsibility to prevent foreign adversaries from acquiring, through investment, theft, academic transfer, or market manipulation, technology that would provide them a decisive military, intelligence, or economic advantage over the United States. 7.2 — Export controls. Congress shall maintain a system of export controls on technologies with significant national security implications. The executive branch shall administer export controls through an independent body whose decisions are based on objective security criteria. Export control decisions shall be reviewed annually and updated to reflect technological developments. Allies and partners may be granted preferential treatment in export licensing consistent with treaty obligations. 7.3 — Technology transfer through academic and research institutions. The executive branch shall maintain a program to identify and mitigate foreign adversary efforts to acquire sensitive technology through academic partnerships, research collaborations, and talent recruitment programs. Federal funding for research in sensitive technology areas shall be conditioned on recipient institutions maintaining appropriate controls on foreign participation. This Section shall be implemented in a manner that does not restrict legitimate international scientific collaboration or discriminate against researchers on the basis of national origin. 7.4 — Supply chain security. The federal government shall assess and mitigate national security risks in the supply chains for critical technology, defense systems, and critical infrastructure. Congress shall by law establish requirements for supply chain security assessment as a condition of federal procurement. Equipment from foreign adversary manufacturers shall not be used in federal systems or designated critical infrastructure where the Accountability Commission's technical review finds a material security risk. 7.5 — No targeting of persons by national origin. Nothing in this Article authorizes surveillance, investigation, restriction of employment, or any adverse action against any person solely on the basis of national origin, ethnicity, or country of birth. Technology security measures must be based on specific and articulable security concerns, not on demographic characteristics. Racial or ethnic profiling in the implementation of this Article is prohibited.

docs.title: Article XVIII — Social, Economic, and Affirmative Rights
docs.summary: Establishes social, economic, environmental, and affirmative rights of all persons within the United States. These rights impose positive duties on government to maintain constitutional floors of protection against destitution, exclusion, and preventable harm inconsistent with a minimally dignified life. They complement the civil and political rights in Article V. The general principles of Article V Section 1, including the rights floor, the prohibition on permanent suspension, absolute rights, and the rule that enumeration does not deny unenumerated rights, apply equally to the rights in this Article. Judicial enforcement under this Article is limited to deciding whether government has met the constitutional floor, whether government has impermissibly backslid from that floor, and what timely lawful remedy is required. Courts may not govern program design in the place of the political branches.
docs.search_text: Article XVIII — Social, Economic, and Affirmative Rights Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Establishes social, economic, environmental, and affirmative rights of all persons within the United States. These rights impose positive duties on government to maintain constitutional floors of protection against destitution, exclusion, and preventable harm inconsistent with a minimally dignified life. They complement the civil and political rights in Article V. The general principles of Article V Section 1, including the rights floor, the prohibition on permanent suspension, absolute rights, and the rule that enumeration does not deny unenumerated rights, apply equally to the rights in this Article. Judicial enforcement under this Article is limited to deciding whether government has met the constitutional floor, whether government has impermissibly backslid from that floor, and what timely lawful remedy is required. Courts may not govern program design in the place of the political branches. Section 1 — Environmental Rights 1.1 Every person has the right to a healthy environment, including clean air, safe drinking water, and freedom from toxic contamination. 1.2 Every person has the right to a stable climate. The government has a duty to adopt and maintain reasonable measures, consistent with the best available scientific evidence, to prevent dangerous interference with the climate system and to protect present and future generations from the consequences of climate change. Government may not knowingly and materially dismantle existing climate protections without replacing them with measures that are constitutionally sufficient in aggregate. A court finding a violation of this duty may declare the breach, require the political branches to adopt a constitutionally sufficient remedial plan within a defined period, and retain jurisdiction to determine whether the submitted plan meets the constitutional floor. A court may not prescribe emissions targets, technology choices, tax measures, or line item appropriations. 1.3 In all decisions affecting the environment, the government shall give weight to the interests of future generations who cannot participate in current political processes. 1.4 The rights in this Section create enforceable duties on government. A court may determine whether government action or inaction is plainly inadequate to the constitutional floor established by this Section, may declare a violation, may require the submission of a remedial plan within a defined period, and may enforce compliance against executive officials charged with carrying out that plan. Courts shall not prescribe specific policy instruments, manage ongoing environmental programs, or set budget levels except where necessary to prevent the executive from disregarding an existing lawful appropriation. Section 2 — Affirmative Rights 2.1 Healthcare. Every person has the right to access basic healthcare and emergency medical treatment. No person shall be denied emergency medical treatment due to inability to pay. Congress shall establish and maintain a system under which inability to pay does not bar access to medically necessary care. 2.2 Education. Every person has the right to free public education through the completion of secondary school. Public education shall be of sufficient quality to enable meaningful participation in civic and economic life. No state may provide substantially inferior education to any group of students on the basis of race, income, disability, immigration status, or geographic location. 2.3 Housing security. Every person has the right to protection against arbitrary homelessness. Government shall maintain measures sufficient to prevent destitution and to preserve access to basic shelter for persons who would otherwise be left without it through circumstances beyond their control. This right does not guarantee any specific dwelling, but it does require a constitutionally adequate shelter and homelessness prevention floor. 2.4 Enforcement of affirmative rights. The rights in this Section impose duties on government that are judicially enforceable. A court finding a violation may declare that the constitutional floor has not been met, require Congress or the relevant state legislature to adopt a lawful remedy within a defined period, order the executive to use existing lawful authority in the interim, and retain jurisdiction to determine whether the adopted remedy is constitutionally sufficient. Courts may not design a program, choose among materially sufficient policy alternatives, order tax increases, or compel new appropriations as a primary remedy. Government may not deliberately dismantle an existing system of protection below the constitutional floor unless it simultaneously replaces it with protections that are materially equal or greater in practical effect. If the political branches fail to act in good faith by the court's deadline, the court may preserve existing protections against backsliding and enforce compliance orders against responsible executive officials. Section 3 — Economic Rights 3.1 Right to work. Every person has the right to work and to pursue a lawful occupation free from arbitrary government restriction. Licensing requirements for occupations must be rationally related to the protection of public health, safety, or welfare. 3.2 Right to organize. Every worker has the right to form, join, and be represented by a labor union or other collective bargaining organization, and to engage in collective bargaining. No law shall prohibit collective bargaining or penalize workers for organizing. 3.3 Economic security. Congress shall maintain programs ensuring a basic standard of living for persons unable to support themselves due to age, disability, illness, caregiving burden, or economic hardship. Such protections may not be eliminated or materially reduced without replacement by protections that are constitutionally sufficient and materially equal or greater in practical effect. 3.4 Fair compensation. Every worker has the right to a living wage: compensation sufficient to meet basic needs including food, housing, healthcare, and transportation in the region where they work. Congress shall establish a minimum wage floor meeting this standard, set the specific amount by law, and review and adjust it at regular intervals sufficient to prevent erosion below the constitutional floor. States may set higher minimums. Congress may not freeze, delay, or reduce the wage floor below what this Section requires. Section 4 — Rights of Children 4.1 — Children as rights holders. Children are rights holders under this Constitution. The rights enumerated in this Article and in Article V apply to children as well as adults. Where rights require modification in their application to children by reason of age and developing capacity, such modification shall be made in the child's best interests, not for the convenience of the state or other adults. 4.2 — Right to education. Every child has the right to free public education of sufficient quality to develop their intellectual and civic capacities. No child may be denied education on the basis of their race, national origin, disability, immigration status, gender, or the circumstances of their parents. 4.3 — Protection from abuse, neglect, and exploitation. Every child has the right to protection from physical abuse, sexual abuse, emotional abuse, neglect, and economic exploitation. The state has an affirmative duty to protect children from these harms, including harms inflicted by parents, guardians, and institutions. 4.4 — Protection from child labor. No child under sixteen years of age may be employed in work that interferes with their education, endangers their health, or is hazardous in nature. Congress shall define and enforce standards protecting children from exploitative labor. Light, non hazardous work by older children that does not interfere with education is not prohibited. 4.5 — Right to be heard. In any legal proceeding that directly affects a child's welfare — including custody, adoption, guardianship, and child protective proceedings — the child's views shall be given weight appropriate to the child's age and maturity. A child has the right to independent representation in any proceeding in which custody, placement, or comparable fundamental interests are at issue. Where a court departs materially from a mature child's expressed preference, it shall state specific reasons on the record grounded in the child's welfare. 4.6 — Best interests standard. In all actions by government affecting children, the best interests of the child shall be a primary consideration. This standard applies to legislative, executive, and judicial action and may not be displaced by administrative convenience, blanket stigma, or categorical assumptions about family status. 4.7 — Children of incarcerated parents. The incarceration of a parent does not, by itself, constitute grounds for the permanent termination of parental rights. The state shall make reasonable efforts to maintain family bonds where doing so is in the child's best interests.

docs.title: Article XIX — Ratification and Transition
docs.summary: Governs ratification, legal continuity, supersession of the prior constitutional order, first-cycle implementation, and transition to the institutions established by this Constitution. This Article is read to favor prompt, lawful, and democratically legitimate establishment of the new constitutional order.

headings.text: Section 3 — Carried-Forward and Superseded Constitutional Provisions

docs.search_text: Article XIX — Ratification and Transition Status: [ DRAFT ] Scope Governs ratification, legal continuity, supersession of the prior constitutional order, first cycle implementation, and transition to the institutions established by this Constitution. This Article is read to favor prompt, lawful, and democratically legitimate establishment of the new constitutional order. Section 1 — Ratification 1.1 This Constitution shall take effect upon ratification by conventions or legislatures in three fourths of the states, as Congress shall determine by law. 1.2 Ratification under this Section supersedes the prior Constitution of the United States except as expressly carried forward by this Article or by other provisions of this Constitution. 1.3 Upon ratification, all federal and state officers shall remain bound to preserve, protect, and defend this Constitution in place of the prior Constitution. Section 2 — Continuity of Law 2.1 All federal statutes, treaties, regulations, judicial judgments, and lawful obligations in force at the time of ratification remain in force unless they conflict with this Constitution, are superseded by laws enacted under it, or expire by their own terms. 2.2 Existing offices, departments, courts, and agencies continue temporarily insofar as necessary to preserve legal continuity, but must be conformed to this Constitution within the periods provided by law or by this Article. 2.3 No existing law shall be given effect after ratification in a manner inconsistent with any right, structural rule, or institutional limitation established by this Constitution. Section 3 — Carried Forward and Superseded Constitutional Provisions 3.1 Rights protections from the prior Constitution that are consistent with this Constitution are carried forward only to the extent incorporated into Article V or otherwise restated here. 3.2 All prior constitutional provisions governing the structure, powers, composition, selection, terms, succession, or procedures of the federal branches are superseded by Articles I through IV and Articles VII through XVIII of this Constitution. 3.3 All prior constitutional provisions inconsistent with direct national election of the President, the abolition of the Electoral College, the Regional Assembly, fixed Supreme Court terms, the Constitutional Organs, or the amendment framework of this Constitution are superseded. 3.4 Congress shall by law publish an official transition schedule identifying the principal prior constitutional provisions carried forward, superseded, or restated, but the legal effect of this Constitution does not depend on that publication. 3.5 No officer, court, or agency may use uncertainty about transition to preserve a superseded constitutional mechanism once this Constitution has made the contrary rule effective by its own terms. Section 4 — First Elections and Terms 4.1 Congress shall by law provide for the first federal elections held under this Constitution and for the orderly commencement of terms under the new constitutional order. 4.2 The first elections under this Constitution must be conducted no later than one year after ratification unless this Constitution expressly provides otherwise. 4.3 Holders of offices abolished or materially transformed by this Constitution shall continue temporarily only for the time strictly necessary to transfer authority to the offices created by this Constitution. 4.4 Congress shall by law provide initial staggering, temporary seat allocation, and transitional succession arrangements necessary to establish the House of Representatives, the Regional Assembly, and the new election system, but no such law may alter the permanent constitutional design. 4.5 If Congress fails to provide by law for a first federal election on the timetable required by this Constitution, the Electoral Commission shall administer the election under interim rules consistent with this Constitution and subject to expedited judicial review. Section 5 — Judicial and Institutional Transition 5.1 Congress shall by law provide for the transition from the prior federal judiciary to the judicial structure established by Article IV, including the sequencing of terms, temporary service, the commencement of the Article IV appointment schedule, the constitution of the Judicial Nominations Commission, and the establishment of lower court terms consistent with that Article. 5.2 The Constitutional Organs established by Article XII shall be constituted as soon as practicable after ratification and shall assume their constitutional functions on the timeline provided by law, but no later than one year after ratification unless impossible due to conditions identified publicly by the Supreme Court. 5.2A If the deadline in Section 5.2 is missed and no impossibility finding has been identified publicly by the Supreme Court, the Court shall order completion of the applicable appointment process within 30 days. If that period also expires without constitution of the affected Organ, a majority of the Supreme Court may appoint temporary Commissioners meeting the qualifications of Article XII in the number strictly necessary to create a lawful quorum and permit the Organ to begin performing its startup, continuity, and protective functions. Temporary Commissioners may exercise only those powers necessary to begin lawful operation and preserve constitutional continuity, and their appointments expire when the ordinary appointment process is completed or after 180 days, whichever comes first, unless renewed once by written judicial findings that completion remains unlawfully delayed despite good faith efforts. 5.3 Existing agencies and officers whose functions are reassigned under this Constitution shall continue only until those functions are lawfully transferred, replaced, or terminated consistent with this Constitution. 5.4 Congress shall by law establish the initial terms, appointments, and temporary staffing needed to bring Articles XII through XV into operation without interrupting the lawful functioning of the government. Section 6 — Electoral and Executive Transition 6.1 The lawful constitutional transfer of power to officers elected or confirmed under this Constitution is mandatory. No outgoing official may obstruct the establishment of the constitutional order or use the powers of an expiring office to frustrate transition. Branch specific transfer duties remain applicable where separately stated in this Constitution. 6.2 Congress shall provide public funding, access, records, security, and administrative support sufficient for constitutional transition. 6.3 Obstruction of the ratified constitutional transition through force, fraud, abuse of official authority, or deliberate refusal to transfer lawful power is anti subversion and grounds for removal, disqualification, and prosecution. 6.4 No outgoing officer may use emergency authority, appointment power, classified control, fiscal control, or control of records to frustrate the lawful implementation of this Constitution after ratification. 6.5 No outgoing officer or federal employee may deliberately destroy, alter, conceal, or transfer outside lawful custody any federal record, communication, or official document during a constitutional transition. Deliberate destruction or concealment of public records during transition is anti subversion conduct under Article VI Section 1.1 and grounds for prosecution by the Accountability Commission. This provision applies to all federal records and is independent of the protections for electoral records in Article VI Section 1.2(c). Section 7 — Temporary Corrective Legislation 7.1 For ten years after ratification, Congress may enact corrective legislation under this Section only to address a genuine procedural gap, a direct textual conflict, or a missing sequencing rule arising from implementation of this Constitution. Corrective legislation may not expand any power, restrict any right, modify any structural provision beyond the identified defect, or address matters left to ordinary legislation. Each corrective act must identify with specificity the constitutional defect it addresses. The authority of this Section expires ten years after ratification and may not be extended. 7.2 The Supreme Court shall have expedited jurisdiction to review any claim that purported corrective legislation exceeds the authority of this Section. Any ambiguity about whether an act qualifies under this Section shall be resolved against the legislation. 7.3 Corrective legislation under this Section may not be used to preserve a superseded office, re create a superseded veto point, postpone a right, election, or institutional safeguard whose effective date is fixed by this Constitution, or fill any gap that this Constitution itself addresses through a self executing default rule. Section 8 — First Amendment Cycle 8.1 No Track 1 structural amendment may be proposed until ten years after ratification except to correct technical errors or conflicts identified during implementation. This cooling off period allows the structural constitutional order to stabilize before revision begins. 8.2 Track 2 rights amendments remain permitted immediately on the timetable established by Article XI.
